data.title: JP Morgan boss warns Burnham against bank tax
data.description:
The boss of JP Morgan warned Andy Burnham against launching a tax raid on banks as he urged the new Prime Minister to focus on growth.

Jamie Dimon said there would be “adverse consequences” if Mr Burnham targeted lenders with fresh windfall taxes to help fund his spending plans.

He said: “It would be one more negative on that bucket of things you got to think about.

“I always thought it was wrong. We’re a great citizen [in the UK]. We hire people there. We want to be bigger there. We train people there. We hire veterans there.

“So if a government decides to do it, there’s nothing I can do, but it will over time cause decisions made that they may not like.”

Banks operating in the UK currently face a 3pc annual charge on top of corporation tax. This was brought down from a previous level of 8pc by Rishi Sunak.

But City chiefs are bracing for a potential increase in their tax bill after union bosses called for Mr Burnham to reverse the cut to the so-called bank surcharge to help fund his energy-bills support package.

Mr Dimon has previously threatened to scrap JP Morgan’s plans to build new £3bn headquarters in Canary Wharf if Labour lurched to the Left.

Speaking on The Master Investor Podcast, the bank boss declined to say whether an increase in taxes would put the plans in jeopardy, saying: “I don’t know what I’d do.”

But he added: “I want London to be our happy home for a long time. I would be very cautious if I was a government thinking that penalising any company out of the ordinary is a good thing for that country.”

The Trades Union Congress (TUC), which represents the majority of the UK’s organised trade union movement, has urged Mr Burnham to restore the bank surcharge to its previous level of 8pc, arguing that doing so would raise £9bn in four years.

Paul Nowak, the general secretary of the TUC, said last week: “The big banks are making a killing off the back of higher interest rates and mortgage misery across the country. They can well afford to pay more tax.”

However, Lord Jim O’Neill, a former Goldman Sachs chief economist who has been helping Mr Burnham prepare his economic agenda, has poured cold water on the suggestions.

He has suggested that the new Prime Minister should balance the books through lower spending rather than tax rises.

‘I’m praying Labour get policy right’

Mr Dimon said he wanted to see Mr Burnham succeed and the UK thrive. He added that the new Chancellor – named on Monday night as John Healey – would need to implement “policies that actually cause growth”.

The JP Morgan boss said: “Good policies are free and growth is the best antidote. Growth also helps the lowest paid people the most.

“So I’m praying that they get policy right, and government after government get it wrong.”

Mr Dimon pointed to the recent exodus of companies from the London stock market as further evidence of the harm caused by high taxes.

He said: “If you have an uncompetitive tax system, capital leaves your country. And if capital leaves your country, it goes to other countries.

“How many companies have delisted from London in the last couple of years? I wouldn’t want to see that if I was running a country.”

Flutter, the gambling group that owns Paddy Power and Betfair, last month became the latest company to say it would abandon its listing on the London Stock Exchange.

CRH, the building materials group, delisted from London earlier this year in favour of a switch to New York. Wise, the £8bn fintech company which was founded in London in 2011, also moved its main listing to the US in May.

Mr Dimon’s comments came after JP Morgan last week reported the highest quarterly profit ever for a US bank thanks to a record performance by its stock traders and a one-off $4.6bn (£3.4bn) boost from its long-held stake in Visa.

But Mr Dimon warned that “several risks are shifting below the surface like tectonic plates”, which could cause “meaningful disruptions when they shift or collide”.

Adding to those remarks on the podcast, he said: “You have wars in Ukraine, terrorism in the Middle East, obviously Iran, great global deficits, remilitarisation in the world, America’s relation with China.

“So there are a lot of things out there which could cause a problem, but again, they might not.”

data.title: Tesla and Google’s AI splurge sparks tech sell-off
data.description:
Technology giants suffered a brutal sell-off on Thursday after heavy AI spending by Tesla and Google spooked investors.

Elon Musk’s Tesla suffered one of its biggest ever share price drops, falling by more than 12pc after the company’s profits were hit by investments in AI.

Google’s parent company, Alphabet, dropped by almost 7pc after it burned through billions in cash to fund its own AI spending.

A surging oil price that raised the prospect of interest-rate hikes added fuel to the fire, prompting the Nasdaq composite, a basket of tech shares, to plunge 2.6pc in its worst day for almost two months.

Investors have become nervous about heavy spending on AI as the technology is yet to generate reliable profits. Concerns have been heightened by the recent launch of Kimi K3, a cheaper Chinese AI that could rival US models.

News of Google and Tesla’s spending led to sell-offs at Amazon, Meta, Microsoft and Oracle, which are all making substantial AI investments.

Tesla revealed a fall in profits on Wednesday night despite the company selling more cars than a year ago.

Although revenues rose, substantial discounts on its cars ate into profit margins. The company also made less money from regulatory credits, which the electric-car maker sells to other manufacturers that fail to hit emissions targets.

Donald Trump has cancelled electric-car subsidies and dismantled the regime that allowed Tesla to sell emissions credits.

As its electric-vehicle business comes under pressure, the company is spending billions on expanding production of its driverless Cybercab and Optimus robots, as well as grid batteries and solar panels.

“This is the fastest industrial scale-up since World War II in America,” Mr Musk told analysts.

Mr Musk became the world’s first trillionaire after his rocket company SpaceX went public earlier this year, but the company’s shares have since slumped. He has a 30pc stake in Tesla, which declined in value by more than $50bn (£37.5bn) on Thursday.

Meanwhile, Google’s parent company Alphabet warned investors that it could spend more than $200bn on AI this year, the second time it has raised forecasts.

Anat Ashkenazi, its chief financial officer, told investors that capital expenditure would be between $195bn and $205bn this year. Much of it will be spent on AI data centres and the chips that run AI applications.

This was up from a prior estimate of $180bn to $190bn, which itself was an increase on an earlier forecast of $175bn to $185bn. Ms Ashkenazi said the increase was to meet growing demand and that spending would jump again next year.

The company burned through $5.9bn of cash during the three months to June – the first time that cash flow had been negative in Google’s two decades as a public company.

data.title: Housebuilders suffer worst run of profit warnings since financial crisis
data.description:
Britain’s housebuilders are issuing profit warnings at the same rate as during the depths of the 2008 financial crisis as the sector buckles under the strain of tax rises and faltering buyer confidence.

Builders and construction companies on the London Stock Exchange issued eight profit warnings in the first half of 2026, including six in the second quarter alone, according to an audit by EY’s strategy consulting division.

Barring the pandemic-hit first half of 2020, it marks the worst total recorded in the first six months of a year since 2008.

Tim Vance, a partner at EY-Parthenon’s UK and Ireland arm, said that higher energy and building costs, weaker buyer confidence and “fading expectations” of further interest rate cuts had all taken their toll on the sector.

Planning delays, regulatory complexity, environmental constraints and persistent skills shortages have also weighed on activity.

“The market conditions right now and in the near term are really challenging,” said Mr Vance.

The wave of profit warnings comes as homebuilders warn that strict regulations, lengthy council planning disputes and affordable home targets are making it unprofitable to build new homes.

Rob Perrins, the boss of Berkeley Group, warned earlier this year that it was becoming impossible to build in London because of high taxes and red tape.

He said: “You can’t keep increasing taxation on fewer and fewer houses.”

Housebuilding in London has fallen sharply as high costs mean construction companies are struggling to meet even watered-down targets for affordable housing.

The 2017 Grenfell fire, which killed 72 people, triggered a new wave of building safety regulations and costs.

From April 2022, large builders have had to pay a residential property developer tax to fund the country’s cladding remediation bill.

In October this year, developers will also have to pay the building safety levy, another tax to pay for cladding remediation. Environmental rules have also tightened, piling on further costs.

Those that have cut their profit guidance so far this year include Crest Nicholson, Berkeley Group and Taylor Wimpey. Last week, Crest Nicholson said it swung to a loss for the first half of its financial year.

Several companies have also scaled back plans to buy land for construction projects to offset weak demand and boost their cash reserves. Vistry, which is one of the builders that has done this, has been cutting house prices at some of its sites to bolster sales.

“Questions around liquidity, covenant headroom and restructuring options will move further up the sector agenda,” Mr Vance predicted as he said balance sheet strength was “becoming an increasingly important differentiator”.

Crest Nicholson is attempting to secure new terms attached to its debt. It has secured a temporary waiver to terms while discussions are ongoing.

The cost of building a new home has risen by £76,000 since 2020 according to the Home Builders Federation. Costs have been pushed up by thousands of pounds in extra taxes such as the building safety levy, as well as inflation in labour and building material costs.

Listed housebuilders have issued 47 profit warnings since the start of 2020, according to EY’s research, almost double the 27 recorded during the prior 13 years combined.

More broadly, UK-listed businesses issued 59 profit warnings in the second quarter of 2026, up from 55 in the preceding three months. Almost a fifth of all UK-listed businesses have issued at least one profit warning in the past 12 months.

More than half of companies that issued warnings in the second quarter blamed policy changes and geopolitical uncertainty, the biggest proportion EY has recorded since it began tracking the data more than 25 years ago. Two in five cited the impact of the Iran war.

data.title: Air con spending spree triggers unexpected boost for retailers
data.description:
Retail sales rose unexpectedly in June after Britons rushed to buy air conditioning units and fans during last month’s heatwave.

Figures showed that retail sales increased 1pc last month, beating expectations of a 0.5pc slump, according to the Office for National Statistics.

The warm weather and summer discounts also encouraged consumers to restock their summer wardrobe as sales in clothing stores rose by 1.9pc in June, the largest jump in nine months.

This was alongside unprecedented demand for air con, with retailers such as Currys reporting sales increases of more than 300pc.

Jacqui Baker, partner and head of retail at RSM UK, said: “The uptick in retail sales highlights just how important weather is in determining consumers’ moods and their willingness to spend, which was also helped by summer discounting. 

“Consumers opted to spend online to avoid the heat on the high street, stocking up on summer clothing to enjoy the hot weather at home, while investing in fans and aircon units to keep cool.”

Retailers also received a boost from shoppers buying more football shirts for the World Cup.

As Britons opted to spend more time outside during the spell of warm weather, retailers reported an increase in customers purchasing garden furniture.

However, despite the rise in retail sales, shoppers steered clear of the high street as soaring temperatures led to Britons doing more shopping online.

This pushed the proportion of digital sales up to 29.4pc in June, a rise from 28.9pc in May. It marked the highest proportion of sales made online since April 2021, when the UK was grappling with the pandemic.

Sandra Prince, analyst at Lloyds, said: “While the weather may have put some shoppers off heading to the high street, many simply switched to online with strong digital sales helping to maintain momentum for retailers.”

However, economists warned that the rise in retail sales is unlikely to continue in the coming months as the energy price shock from the Iran war is expected to weigh on consumers.

Harvir Dhillon, economist at the British Retail Consortium, said: “While retailers have been enjoying the boost to sales, there are challenges ahead, particularly as hostilities in the Middle East resume.

“Household budgets remain under pressure, consumer confidence is fragile, and retailers are facing rising operating costs.”

data.title: Claudia Winkleman quits BBC chat show after one series
data.description:
Claudia Winkleman has quit her BBC chat show after one series, saying she was “just too nervous to enjoy it”.

The Claudia Winkleman Show launched in March and was expected to be a hit following the presenter’s success as host of The Traitors and Strictly Come Dancing.

But the programme failed to land with viewers and the presenter seemed uncomfortable with the format. She also suffered from a lack of A-list guests.

“Sometimes you have to try something to see how it fits, and I realised I was just too nervous to enjoy it,” said Winkleman.

“Maybe one day I will give it another try, but for now I already have the best jobs in the world and absolutely love the shows I’m doing.”

Winkleman told the Daily Mail: “I’m incredibly grateful to the BBC for giving me the opportunity, to the guests who agreed to come and chat to me, and the production team who were simply excellent.”

Ed Havard, the director of entertainment at the BBC, said: “Claudia’s warmth and quick wit made The Claudia Winkleman Show an absolute joy.

“Whilst we loved the show, we fully respect her decision and would like to thank Claudia and the brilliant team at So Television for bringing the series to screen on the BBC.

“As the host of the country’s most-watched show, Claudia will be back on the BBC very soon when The Celebrity Traitors returns later this year, and we can’t wait to see what she has in store for the new band of celebs.”

The show ran for only seven episodes. It was made by So Television, Graham Norton’s production company.

During its seven-week run in the spring, Winkleman interviewed guests including as Lisa Kudrow, Jeff Goldblum, Niall Horan and Rachel Zegler.

Ralph Fiennes, James McAvoy, Jamie Dornan, Vanessa Williams and incoming Strictly co-host Josh Widdicombe were among the other guests on the sofa.

Most episodes attracted an average audience of 1.2million viewers.

The programme divided critics. Some described it as entertaining and “full of banter”, but others suggesting it was “messy” and “a bit of a bore”.

In an interview with the Daily Mail’s weekend magazine before the first show, she said she was unsure if she had the “vim” required to be a good celebrity chat show presenter.

She said: “I’m just not sure I’ll be very good at this, that’s the truth.”

“I like napping. I’m low on vim. So, yeah, it’s problematic. I’ll tell you what it’s going to be. This is your headline: “A Disaster”.’

She said she had initially believed The Traitors would not be a success, saying: “I phoned my husband from the Scottish castle, holding ceremonial pouches and red leather fingerless gloves I’d got off Amazon, and said, “I’ve had a good run, this is where it comes to an end.” This is also how I feel about this show.’ Of course, The Traitors was and is a massive hit.”

In 2022, Norton cut the number of episodes of his chat show he makes every year, dropping the spring run in favour of a shorter series which airs over the autumn and winter.

His show generally attracts an audience of between 2.5million and 3million viewers, although some episodes such as the New Year’s Eve special can score up to 4million.

Winkleman took the job after leaving Strictly Come Dancing. She continues to present The Traitors and its celebrity spin-off, plus The Piano on Channel 4.

She presented Strictly spin-off show It Takes Two from 2004 until 2014, when she took over from Bruce Forsyth as a lead presenter.

data.title: The new breed of European-made small electric cars countering cheap Chinese EVs
data.description:
Small is beautiful, but the once-strong market for diminutive cars – beloved of older drivers and those just starting out – has dried up in the last decade as burgeoning safety and environmental legislation along with negligible profits have sounded the death knell for the genre.

This is a crying shame. While our leaders and legislators might barely see, let alone own and drive, small cars, there’s still a ready market for them, with most folk nurturing their old models as they wonder how they will replace them.

That’s especially so in Rome, home of the small car, where drivers cherish a variety of battered piccole auto from all over the globe. As my Roman waiter pointed out: “The streets, they don’t get any bigger.”

Late and lamented are: Volkswagen’s Up range, including the electric e-Up, withdrawn in 2013, Peugeot and Citroën’s 108 and C1 twins (both in 2022), the Ford Fiesta (2023), the Smart Fortwo (2024), the Fiat Panda (2024), and many others.

You can still buy a brand new Fiat Panda from £9,870 in Italy, which perhaps raises a few questions about UK car pricing. And undoubtedly opportunities to innovate have been squandered, even by Fiat, such as with its Ecobasic concept, which combined an aluminium chassis and plastic bodywork for a weight of only 750kg, and a small diesel that gave 97mpg economy.

Those vanished models, along with rising prices for the few city cars left on sale, are indicative of the tough task of balancing size, price, range, weight and practicality, along with the difficulty of meeting the safety regulations. Several European firms have told me that 20 to 25 per cent of their entire engineering staff are permanently deployed on legislative requirements regarding the environment and safety.

Small EVs offer hope

Toyota, Kia, Hyundai and Suzuki are still committed to the UK market with petrol models, but does hope for the next generation of small cars lie with a new range of electric vehicles (EVs)?

I have driven the new, sub-£20,000 electric Renault Twingo (which in a deal of dubious merit will soon metamorphose into a new Ford Fiesta) and liked it. Citroën’s electric revival of the 2CV is likely to be revealed at the Paris motor show in October with a mooted price of about £15,000, while Volkswagen’s VW’s ID.1, described as “the last piece of the puzzle” by VW’s chief executive Thomas Schäfer, is due next year at around £17,000. It’s fair to say that improving battery technology will provide these tiddlers with at least 150 miles of range; these are proper cars, but at a price. Only 10 years ago, a basic new Ford Fiesta cost only £10,250.

A couple of other possibilities could auger a return of small cars. Tiny Kei-class cars constitute one of Japan’s best-selling markets; no more than 3.4m long, with an engine limit of 660cc and 63bhp, they are certainly cheap and (even in EV form) are great fun to drive. Electric versions have decent ranges of between 150 and 190 miles, but engineering them to pass crash tests designed for gargantuan three-ton SUVs requires costly amounts of work. Honda’s Super-N, a souped-up city car, is based on a Kei-class machine, but it costs £19,000 and has a range of only 128 miles.

New regulations

Hence, manufacturers are lobbying for separate safety regulations for small, low-speed city cars. There is an EU proposal for a new M1E class for EVs under 4.2m long; Citroën is hoping to slot its new 2CV into this as-yet-unconfirmed legislative category.

Quadricycles present another possibility for circumventing the rulebook. The European standard is called L7; to qualify, a car must have a kerb weight of no more than 400kg (if an EV, that’s without the battery), develop no more than 20.1bhp and have a top speed no greater than 56mph. Crash tests of deformable structures are not required.

In Europe, such vehicles can be driven by 16-year-olds with a B1 driving licence. Not that this will be the case in the UK, which post-Brexit might have undertaken to adopt EU regulations but doesn’t necessarily adopt the ideas wholeheartedly, so driving a quadricycle requires a full licence.

Citroën and Fiat set the pace

Citroën’s Ami quadricycle generated plenty of interest, but it hasn’t been the most successful vehicle in the UK, hampered by its £7,695 price, 46-mile range and, at absolute best, a 28mph top speed (which falls to walking pace uphill). The unpainted, plastic two-seater had all the glamour of a holiday in North Korea, and a commitment to a low price was all too evident.

“Then Carlos [Tavares, former Stellantis boss] asked if Fiat wanted to do a version,” says Gaetano Thorel, head of Fiat in Europe. “He thought we’d just put a badge on the front, but we did it our way.”

That way has been to devote a lot of attention to the tiny car, but sadly no upgrade to its drivetrain, to create the Topolino. The Vatican approves, however, with the Governorate of the Vatican City State accepting 30 Topolino and three-wheeled Tris EV delivery vehicles in June, apparently “reinforcing a shared commitment to more sustainable and responsible mobility” for the Holy See. We journalists had a test drive around the Vatican, which was surely a first. However, while these little plastic-bodied cars are charming, the driving experience isn’t, with a terrible ride quality along with faltering performance on Rome’s precipitous hills.

Four-seater version

But Fiat hasn’t finished yet, and in something of a turf war with fellow Stellantis brand Citroën, it showed early impressions of a four-seater version of the Topolino called Multiplina, which, as well as being larger, has “more performance”, according to Thorel. At less than 4.2m long, it limbo dances under the quadricycle regulations.

Citing the “missing link between the Topolino and the traditional [combustion-engined] city car”, Thorel admits that the Multiplina will need a larger battery and greater performance, but it will be a microcar (quadricycle) and “will be for urban areas not highways.

“We made a conscious decision to go all in on micromobility,” he says. “We are at 63.45 per cent of the micro market even in Italy and I’m happy that we are forcing our competitors to do that too.”

He said he thought it unfair that sales of microcars are not counted to offset sales of combustion-engined cars by the EU (or UK) in terms of zero-emission-mandate fines, which “is probably why a lot of manufacturers have tried and quit in this market”. The tandem two-seat Renault Twizy was just such a project, much liked by owners but barely profitable.

The Telegraph verdict

Microcars, the proposed M1E class, Kei cars, quadricycles (not to mention e-scooters and electric bicycles) – the future of micromobility is confusing, as well as highly disputed, territory, whether on two, three or four wheels.

But there are concerns about all of them over crash safety and relative speeds with other vehicles (especially on urban highways), along with users being under the influence of drink or drugs. The UK is seemingly being slow to draw conclusions and embrace the freedom and environmental benefits of even e-scooters (the trial has been extended into the long grass of 2028), let alone quadricycles.

Fiat, though, believes that such modes of personal transport could provide a bulwark against the Chinese firms eager to enter European and UK markets with an armada of tiny vehicles.

The viability of micromobility, especially in congested urban centres, needs to be resolved. In the meantime, European buyers young and old will surely celebrate the renaissance of more affordable personal transport.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shadow bank collapse triggers surge in insolvencies
data.description:
The collapse of the British shadow bank Market Financial Solutions (MFS) amid allegations of fraud has triggered a surge in insolvencies across the financial services sector.

The number of finance companies entering administration in the first half of the year rose by 63pc compared to 2025, according to consultancy Kroll.

Overall, 49 finance firms collapsed in the first half of the year, up from 30 during the same period a year earlier. However, almost half of those that failed in the first half of 2026 were related to the fall of MFS.

The lender skewed the data after the network of companies it used to extend loans also ran into trouble. Sarah Rayment of Kroll said they were “intermediaries or brokers [who] have failed as a consequence of the collapse of MFS”.

MFS was a so-called shadow bank, which meant it did not accept deposits and instead funded its loans by borrowing from banks and other lenders. A shadow bank is a private creditor that is not subject to regulatory oversight.

The company described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance.

It was part of a fast-growing crop of so-called bridging lenders in the UK. 

These firms provide short-term, property-backed loans to borrowers who may not qualify for traditional bank financing and often charge higher interest rates.

MFS fell into administration in February, triggering allegations that at least £1.3bn has been “misappropriated” from the business.

Paresh Raja, its founder, has been accused of “plundering” the company to fund a lavish lifestyle. He is accused of buying “a vast number of cars”, including three Aston Martins, two Mercedes, six Ferraris and three Rolls-Royces.

The allegations have been made in a lawsuit on behalf of some of the shadow bank’s creditors, which include Barclays, Santander, Wells Fargo and Jefferies.

Mr Raja, who lives in Dubai, is subject to a worldwide travel ban and asset freeze. He strongly denies the allegations and has consistently maintained that there was no fraud or dishonesty.

Sarah Rayment, the managing director and global co-head of restructuring at Kroll, said: “This year it’s across financial services where we are seeing more distress.

“However, look closely; it’s not necessarily a systematic issue with the industry, but many of these businesses are intermediaries or brokers [who] have failed as a consequence of the collapse of MFS. As a result, financial compliance is becoming a key topic of conversation among boards.”

Across the economy, 649 companies entered administration between January and June 2026, marking a 6pc increase on the same period in 2025.

The manufacturing, construction and property sectors accounted for the highest absolute numbers of insolvencies, according to Kroll, at 80, 77 and 59, respectively, in the first half of the year.

Some sectors performed better than last year. Administrations in the retail sector were down 23pc, but businesses remained under pressure amid a number of high-profile collapses, including The Original Factory Shop.

data.title: US threatens China with sanctions over claims of AI theft
data.description:
The White House has threatened China with sanctions after accusing one of its top AI companies of stealing American technology.

Scott Bessent, the Treasury Secretary, said claims that Chinese start-up Moonshot had extracted trade secrets from US company Anthropic could trigger retaliatory actions including a new round of levies on Beijing.

Mr Bessent’s intervention came after Michael Kratsios, the director of Donald Trump’s Office of Science and Technology Policy, claimed Moonshot had started “stealing” US technology on an “industrial” scale.

Mr Bessent said: “We support open-source AI and the innovation it unlocks. But open source is not open season on American IP. When (People’s Republic of China) firms conduct covert, industrial-scale distillation attacks that cross the line into IP theft, sanctions and Entity List designations will be on the table.”

The intervention escalates a rivalry between the US and China about AI, and could lead to sanctions on Chinese providers.

Moonshot’s new AI model, Kimi K3, has prompted concerns that Chinese AI is closing the gap with leading US companies, coming close to the technology developed by Anthropic and OpenAI.

However, Mr Kratsios said the company’s success had in part been down to China covertly taking advantage of American technology.

He said Moonshot had used a technique known as “distillation” to extract abilities from Anthropic’s powerful Fable system, as well as circumventing a ban on high-end Nvidia chips.

“We have information that Moonshot AI distilled Anthropic’s Fable for the development of its K3 model,” he wrote on X.

“They developed a sophisticated internal platform to conduct large scale distillation against US models, allowing them to quickly switch between multiple methods of access to avoid detection.

“Moonshot AI has also acquired [Nvidia] GB300-equipped servers and has accessed GB300s in Thailand, likely to train its AI models.”

He added: “Large-scale, covert industrial distillation aimed at stealing proprietary US technology and undermining American research is unacceptable.”

US export controls forbid Chinese companies from obtaining Nvidia’s top chips, even outside the country. The components are seen as a crucial US advantage in the AI race.

Distillation involves developing an AI system using the answers from a more powerful one, allowing it to mimic the powerful system’s abilities. The major AI labs ban the practice, and seek to shut it down when they detect it.

Anthropic said earlier this year that Moonshot had used its models more than 3.4 million times, breaking both its terms of service and geographical restrictions banning Chinese users from accessing it.

Fable, the high-end model allegedly used to train the latest version of Kimi, was only released in June, suggesting the activity had continued since.

Jacob Helberg, a Trump-administration official, called Moonshot’s actions a “heist of invaluable American intellectual property”.

“Lying, cheating and stealing is not innovation or ‘win-win’. It’s corrosive to productive economic activity and to the healthy economic development of nations,” he added.

This appeared to be a reference to Xi Jinping saying that China supported “win-win cooperation” by promoting so-called open-source AI technology, which can be freely downloaded and altered in contrast to leading US “closed” AI systems.

Open source systems also represent a threat to Anthropic and OpenAI’s businesses, which rely on charging premiums for access to what had hitherto been seen as the most advanced AI models.

Dean Ball, an executive at OpenAI, said open source Chinese models were a step towards “full AI communism”.

The White House is reportedly considering stronger controls on Americans using Chinese AI systems.

Concerns have increased in recent weeks because the latest AI systems are capable of carrying out advanced cyber attacks.

Scott Bessent, the US Treasury secretary, said earlier this week that Chinese companies found to have stolen American technology could face sanctions.

“If we see, especially, that overseas models are stealing from our great companies, we have the ability to sanction them because of this theft,” he said.

Moonshot and Anthropic were contacted for comment.

The AI spat came as Google revealed it burned through nearly $6bn (£4.5bn) in its latest quarter as the tech giant bets big on AI.

Parent company Alphabet reported negative cash flow of $5.9bn in the three months to the end of June as it spent heavily on AI-related equipment such as data centres and servers.

The company also increased its forecasts for full-year spending by $15bn to between $195bn and $205bn.

However, Google reported an 82pc jump in cloud revenues to $24.8bn, suggesting that its heavy outlay is being matched by strong demand.

Elon Musk’s Tesla also burned through $1.1bn last quarter as the electric car maker pushes deeper into self-driving technology and robotics, though this was less severe than the $3.3bn forecast by analysts.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Burnham pledges to cap bus fares... but funding descends into farce
data.description:
Andy Burnham’s £2 cap on bus fares has triggered a second major funding row in as many days.

The Prime Minister announced on Wednesday morning that he would cap single bus tickets at £2 from Jan 1 until the end of 2027. Sir Keir Starmer increased the level from £2 to £3 shortly after the 2024 election.

The Government said the estimated £500m cost of the cap would be part-covered by savings on net-zero projects and climate finance. That would include lending money to developing countries for projects to address climate change, rather than handing them the money, as happens currently.

The remaining sum of about £50m would come from Department for Transport funding already set to be spent on buses, it said.

However, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, failed five times during an interview to say whether interest would be due on these loans to developing countries. She said: “The detail will need to be worked out. We wanted to make the announcement today.”

Mr Burnham’s opponents accused him of announcing another policy without knowing where the cash would come from, a day after one of Sir Keir Starmer’s closest allies said Mr Burnham was unveiling an unfunded plan to cut VAT from electricity bills.

Ms Alexander told Sky News: “The detail of it needs to be worked through. But in order for loans to qualify as overseas development assistance, the terms of the loan need to be reasonably favourable to the developing country and it needs to contribute to developing that country’s economy.

“So it makes sense to switch the spending, instead of being a grant it will be a loan, and that frees up money to spend on domestic priorities and to spend on our public transport.”

Challenged on whether poorer countries may be anxious about whether any interest was due, Ms Alexander responded: “So this is quite standard to have loans for international climate finance. As I say, the detail will need to be worked out.”

Pressed for a third time, Ms Alexander said: “I’m not going to sit in your studio and tell you how something will work when the detail hasn’t been worked out yet.”

Asked again, the Transport Secretary said she was “not going to give you the detail”, and when challenged for a fifth time, she said the question of interest on the climate loans would need to be “worked through”.

Speaking to BBC Breakfast later on Wednesday morning, Ms Alexander insisted she was “100 per cent sure” the bus fare cap was fully funded.

However, Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, accused Mr Burnham and his top team of “not actually specifying where the money is going to come from”.

He said: “That is a worrying sign, we need to have a Government which is on top of the public finances, not one that is making lots of spending commitments without explaining where the money is going to come from.”

The Telegraph revealed last month that Mr Burnham wanted to introduce the £2 bus-fare scheme as soon as he took over from Sir Keir.

The announcement marked the second major economic policy to be unveiled by the Prime Minister since he entered No 10 on Monday.

He previously revealed plans to cut VAT on electricity bills worth £850m, which he said would use cash from his predecessor’s abandoned digital ID scheme.

However, the tax cut soon descended into farce when Darren Jones, a close ally of Sir Keir who oversaw the digital ID programme, said the proposal was unfunded.

In a stinging public intervention, Mr Jones told Mr Burnham and John Healey, the new Chancellor, that they would need to find the money for the tax cut at the next Budget.

Capping bus fares at £2 will be badged as an attempt to help people back into work while also helping families with the cost of living. Some city mayors used their powers to keep local fares lower, including in Manchester, where Mr Burnham placed bus services under public control.

Writing for LBC after his policy was announced, Mr Burnham said: “I’ve spoken about the wrong turns Britain has taken over the last 40 years. What we did with our buses is a sorry example. When services were sold off and communities lost a say, fares went up and passenger numbers fell.”

He continued: “The measures we’ve announced this week will help but I know they won’t solve every problem. We need to do more. And we will.

“Later this year, we’ll lay out a 10-year plan to bring down the cost of living, help young people into work and build more council homes. A fairer bus fare is just the start. A fairer Britain is where we all want to be. Let’s bring back hope.”

Andrew Gilligan, Boris Johnson’s former transport adviser, accused Mr Burnham of copying the policy when he was Manchester mayor and Mr Johnson was prime minister in 2022.

In a post on X, he said: “Andy Burnham says the £2 bus fare cap was his idea. It was not. We were working on it from spring 2022, and told Burnham’s team. He decided to pre-empt us by introducing it in September 2022, four months before we did. I’m pretty sure we paid for it, though!”

The new bus fare cap does not cover London, which has a daily cap of £5.25 and a weekly cap of £24.70.

data.title: British data centre owner set for £14bn US takeover
data.description:
A British data centre owner is set to accept a £14bn takeover offer from an American suitor in the latest blow to the London stock market.

Segro, which owns a significant hub of data centres in Slough, said on Wednesday that it would probably agree to a bid from Prologis after the warehousing giant improved its offer for the digital infrastructure business.

If it proceeds, the deal will be the largest takeover of a London-listed company this year.

Segro’s statement came minutes before a deadline for Prologis to either make a final, binding offer or walk away. That deadline has been extended to Aug 12.

The US company tabled what it called its “best and final” offer of £10.32 a share after months of pursuing Segro.

The all-share offer, which also includes a cash alternative if shareholders opt for it, is the fourth proposal Prologis has put to Segro’s board, with the others being rejected for undervaluing the company, according to Segro.

Segro had recently faced shareholder pressure to accept Prologis’s offer, including from shareholders at Norges Bank Investment Management and CCLA Investment Management.

Norges said on Tuesday that the deal “merits consideration” as it encouraged both boards to come to an agreement.

Segro was established as the Slough Trading Company in 1920 when its founders bought a First World War military repair depot and turned some of its old workshops into spaces that customers could rent.

Run by David Sleath, the chief executive, its property portfolio is now valued at £19bn and tenants include Amazon, DHL and Royal Mail.

London’s shrinking stock market

New York-listed Prologis has signalled that it will consider a secondary London listing of its shares after a takeover, which would allow shareholders to maintain some exposure to the combined group if a deal is agreed.

However, the loss of Segro is another blow for London’s shrinking stock market after a string of takeovers.

Outsourcer Mitie agreed to a £3.1bn takeover on Tuesday by private equity-owned rival OCS Group, the 11th takeover of a UK-listed company this year worth more than £1bn, according to AJ Bell.

Engineer Rotork also accepted a £4.1bn takeover offer last week by Swedish firm ABB.

Before these took place, analysts at Peel Hunt estimated that there have been 154 bids for UK companies worth more than £100m since 2023, with a total value of £165bn.

The deals are likely to reignite a debate regarding whether London is struggling to retain its biggest listed companies.

Ministers have introduced measures to boost the City’s competitiveness but overseas investors are spotting opportunities for cut-price acquisitions.

data.title: British Gas customers reckon with AI chatbots as call centre jobs cut
data.description:
British Gas customers face the prospect of talking to AI chatbots rather than humans under plans to cut 1,300 call centre jobs.

Centrica, the owner of British Gas, said it would cut another 800 roles, on top of 500 confirmed last month, under plans to reduce its customer service workforce by 14pc.

It said the overhaul had included the “targeted deployment of AI tools” that would help simplify its customer service operations.

Chris O’Shea, the chief executive of Centrica, insisted the job cuts were being driven by customer behaviour as callers instead chose to use digital bots to answer their queries.

Mr O’Shea said: “What we’re seeing just now is over 90pc of our customers actually use digital channels in the first instance, and we’ve seen a 20pc reduction in customer calls, so this simply reflects the changing customer behaviour.”

Centrica said that its “average contact per customer” had fallen by around a fifth since last year.

“Overall, we’ve got to reflect the changes in customer behaviour,” Mr O’Shea said. “We expect to grow more jobs around our digital interface, and maybe having fewer people on the phones.”

Silver Voices, a campaign group for senior citizens, criticised Centrica’s drive towards chatbots, which it warned might affect vulnerable customers “who may not be able to negotiate AI driven contact systems”.

Dennis Reed, its director, said: “Talking to a machine is not good customer service and these job cuts will be counter-productive for shareholders.

“The more AI is rolled out, the more dangers are identified and a national pause for reflection is required.”

Caroline Abrahams, the charity director at Age UK, added that a “significant minority of older people aren’t online at all”.

She said: “For this group in particular, but really for everyone on some occasions, there will remain a need to speak to a human being.”

A spokesman for Centrica said: “We have been transforming the business for a number of years to ensure we have the right roles in the right places for the future.

“This means making changes to improve efficiency, drive commercial performance and give our customers the service they want.

“At the same time, we continue to invest in the skills where there is demand, including recruiting more engineers to meet growing demand and hiring 500 apprentices this year alone.”

The job losses, which will take place during the next two years, came as the company revealed its adjusted operating profits fell 9.5pc in the first half of the year to £497m.

Centrica’s gas supplies have been disrupted by the Iran war, which sent European gas prices up to a three-year high in March.

Mr O’Shea added: “Volatility across energy markets has created challenges in some parts of our business, and some of our delivery has been slower than we would like.

“However, we have continued to invest with discipline to strengthen our portfolio and support long-term growth.”

The job losses follow news that two ageing nuclear power stations partly owned by Centrica will stay operational until 2030 to help keep Britain’s lights on.

EDF said it had decided to extend the life of the Hartlepool and Heysham 1 plants for an extra two years.

The sites are two of four advanced gas-cooled reactors that have been generating since the 1980s. Centrica owns a 20pc stake in the fleet.

data.title: Despite the gloom, Britain can flourish. Here’s how
data.description:
Looking around Britain today, surely only a fool would be an optimist. The nation slithers towards the abyss. Every Budget announces new punishment beatings for business. The state makes a sarcastic mockery of working hard and being honest.

The utilitarian law of globalisation, that price is the only thing that matters, no longer exerts such a vice-like grip over the fate of nations. For decades we have been told that for a fractional base point of additional GDP growth we should surrender strong communities, open the borders and abandon industry.

But the world has changed since the collapse of confidence in globalisation – and in many ways, it’s changed to our advantage. This is a conversation the UK has been postponing. In this context, I shall offer five reasons why Britain can flourish.

Firstly, we have natural energy resources that Continental Europe envies. 

It is obscene to impose the highest industrial energy prices in Europe when we have centuries of exploitable natural gas reserves, plus coal. Although electricity blackouts are probably now baked into the system, we can ensure they don’t keep recurring into the 2030s. 

I have commended the tiny Social Democratic Party’s plan of an emergency “grid stabilisation” of 60GW of new gas and coal, allowing a Messmer-scale building programme of dozens of new gigawatt reactors to proceed. There is no need to race to nuclear, skimping on safety as some of the ersatz “pro-growth” groups insist. Not when reactors last 70 years. An abundance of cheap energy must be our top priority.

Our second important natural resource is minerals. We have copper and gold in Northern Ireland, lithium and tin in Cornwall, and cobalt and rare earths in Scotland. 

There may be even more: as I reported a year ago, we haven’t conducted a national geophysical survey since the early 1960s. Whitehall is waking up to this with some urgency.

Now the third piece of the puzzle can fall into place: by reviving our refining and processing industries. 

British pragmatism thrives in devising new industrial processes. Our petrochemicals sector was once a world-beater, and our excellent chemistry and chemical engineering departments at universities provide the brains. Alas, thanks to high energy prices and policy indifference, this is a sector on its knees.

“If you don’t have a chemicals industry... then you can’t have pharmaceutical production, aerospace production or car production,” Grant Pearson, chairman of Teesside ethanol company Ensus, told The Telegraph recently.

Fourth, British business continues to command respect abroad. 

When I mention this, some readers assume I am being sentimental or ironic. But I hear it affirmed every week. Our reputation for fair play has endured. 

It is astonishing that successive prime ministers and chancellors, from George Osborne to Rachel Reeves, have looked up to the hucksters of Silicon Valley and American venture capital as our model for how to conduct business. Unlike them, we leave something on the table in a deal. Global customers know this, and like it.

Finally, we can capitalise on the engineering and manufacturing talent that creates world-class technology. 

Dyson and Renishaw and Martin-Baker are world beaters in consumer markets, medical instruments and aviation, respectively. “The Germans fear our engineers,” Marcus Gibson, whose research company monitors the health of our SMEs, likes to say.

Writing on the Dyson company blog recently, Sir James Dyson pointed out that nine out of 10 private sector companies are family firms, and employ more than half of all private sector workers. It shows how Labour’s war on family businesses and private property owners is so spiteful. At such times Labour looks much more like a global lobbying firm for hedge funds than a political operation acting in the British national interest.

These five parts are interlocking gears in a modern industrial society; each benefits from the other. For example, a supply of low-cost novel plastics encourages entrepreneurs to innovate and makes prototyping easier.

An industrial revival is not uncharted territory. None of this requires a Stalinist top-down plan or autarky. Government interventions could be limited to giving refining, processing, manufacturing and engineering companies a 10-year amnesty period from tax. 

Other state measures that could help include getting finance to co-operate, which forces successful companies to sell out to grow; and protecting inheritance and strengthening intellectual property enforcement. Other than that, the state should get out of the way and let markets do their thing.

However, this does require something almost unthinkable today: a shift away from the utilitarian logic of globalisation which the UK has embedded. 

This is what I call “performative laissez-faire”. It is performative in the same way that making international climate change pledges is performative: no one else really cares how virtuous we are.

The overarching utilitarian creed is that the consumer must get the cheapest goods, at the lowest price, no matter what the consequences may be for national security or communities. China boomed, while we hollowed ourselves out. 

Surely this ideology is now broken on the rocks. Some will sneer that reviving industry means “picking winners”, but this is nonsense: the phrase should hold no fear for us anymore. Giving industrial sectors a break from punishing taxes would make a refreshing change from the Government consistently picking losers.

The UK has already tried being the management consultancy to the world. Now it’s time to make stuff again.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Burnham’s industrial dream is bound to disappoint
data.description:
Whatever his weaknesses, Andy Burnham, Britain’s new Prime Minister, undoubtedly has popular appeal.

We’ll find out soon enough whether it translates into sensible policy.

But one thing in which he unarguably excels is hopeful storytelling, or what rather irritatingly has become known as “vibes”. A case in point is his supposed plans for reindustrialisation.

Nobody will seriously disagree with the ambition. Though factory life can be monotonous and even soul destroying, it can also be relatively well paid and dependable.

People yearn for a half-remembered past of horny-handed sons of toil who actually made things rather than just traded them.

Burnham taps powerfully into this sense of longing for a bygone time with his talk of a ten-year plan to bring manufacturing jobs back to Britain’s once-proud industrial heartlands.

It’s a promise shared by other populist leaders, not least Donald Trump, who may have little else in common with Burnham but has made American reindustrialisation one of his trademark ambitions. 

He’s as comfortable breaking bread with union bosses as he is with crypto-billionaires and plutocrats.

The first thing to know about what Burnham might have in mind is that at this stage, he does not have a plan for re-industrialisation at all – it’s merely an aspiration.

Presumably, he does not intend to follow Trump into outright protectionism. As an open, trade-based economy, Britain would have far more to lose from such a policy than to gain.

The second thing to note is that realistically we are talking about at best only tens of thousands of jobs, not hundreds of thousands, let alone millions.

The Prime Minister most associated with UK de-industrialisation, at least among the Labour faithful, is of course Margaret Thatcher.

Huge swathes of UK manufacturing closed during her first years in power, when many companies – exposed by her policies to the full force of international competition – struggled to stay afloat.

But interestingly, she did have an industrial strategy, and she certainly believed that the UK could be a good place for manufacturing investment.

It was just that she did not believe in supporting no hope lame ducks. Instead, she wanted best-in-class, highly competitive, frontier companies. To this end, she was ultimately relatively successful in reinvigorating the UK automotive sector with Japanese implants.

Back to the present. The idea that Britain is no longer a manufacturing economy of any significance is somewhat misleading. In fact, we produce more manufactured goods today in absolute terms than ever before, more even than at the height of Victorian industrial prowess.

But the terms of trade have shifted markedly against manufacturing in favour of services. As a proportion of the economy, manufacturing output has slumped in the past fifty years to less than a third of its previous level.

Most of us can agree that the process has gone too far. It is hard to point to any other advanced economy with such a small manufacturing base relative to GDP, though some come close.

Recent geo-political events have highlighted the importance for economic security alone of making more of our own stuff, and not relying as heavily as we do on imports.

Most of the decline can be explained by the fall in manufacturing prices relative to those of services. Domestic demand for manufactured goods has moreover remained pretty much static, whereas demand for services has soared.

But it is also true that productivity in manufacturing failed to keep pace with many overseas peers. Lack of international competitiveness in manufacturing is a large part of the story.

Over the decades, numerous attempts by numerous governments have been made to reverse or at least mitigate this decline. None of them have succeeded.

Analysis more than ten years ago by Professors Stephen Broadberry and Tim Leunig of the London School of Economics on the effects of public policy on UK manufacturing was damning in its conclusions. Many of them remain as valid today as back then.

Government intervention in the form of promoting “national champions” and nationalisation was in nearly all cases an unmitigated failure, they concluded. Similarly with government purchasing and regional policy, whose effects had been at best erratic.

In only one case – Rolls Royce – did nationalisation result in an obviously successful outcome. The standout failure was British Leyland, but there were many others in aviation, shipbuilding, machine tools, electrical engineering, computers and textiles.

Virtually all the literature on industrial subsidies finds them to be money down the drain. More general campaigns to “buy British” have also had little or no meaningful effect.

More recently, full expensing – allowing companies to fully offset spending on plant and machinery against tax – has likewise failed to shift the dial, though this might have more to do with Brexit than the policy itself.

Few big manufacturers want to invest in the UK as long as the UK’s relationship with Europe remains so uncertain. Better, they figure, to invest within the single market.

So it’s a little bit of a mystery as to why Burnham thinks he can succeed where so many others have failed. In most cases, subsidising factory jobs with taxpayer pounds has proved a complete waste of money.

Last week’s nationalisation of British Steel might be thought a special case in view of the company’s position as a seemingly indispensable defence contractor.

The mystery is rather how the Government allowed these ageing industrial assets to be sold to Chinese interests in the first place. 

But at more than £500,000 per job, based on National Audit Office estimates, the costs of keeping the plants open through to 2028 are off the scale. It would be cheaper simply to pay employees not to work.

None of this is to argue that the UK should give up on manufacturing. What’s left of the manufacturing base tends to be highly competitive, high value and strikingly successful, Rolls Royce being an obvious case in point.

When Tufan Erginbilgiç, its chief executive, demands hundreds of millions in launch aid to re-enter the market for narrow-bodied aircraft engines, he knows he’s pushing at an open door. In aerospace, launch aid comes with the territory. All countries provide it given the chance. You either play the game or you get out of it.

If the Government sticks to its pledge to nearly double the defence budget by 2035, this too is bound to be an important boost to manufacturing employment.

But as for the taxpayer-funded subsidies and bailouts of yesteryear, forget it. Even if Burnham wanted to, he no longer has the money for 1970s-style largesse.

It nearly bankrupted the country back then, and it would do so again if similarly applied today. 

Careful with those “vibes” Mr Burnham. They may work for now, but you can only really disappoint.

data.title: Burnham to launch £1bn raid on Amazon and Asos to pay for pub tax break
data.description:
Andy Burnham is expected to announce a £1bn raid on Amazon and Asos to pay for a pubs tax break.

In a blow to online retailers, the Prime Minister is expected to raise business rates for fulfilment warehouses to pay for cutting rates for pubs, clubs and live music venues by 20pc.

The policy announcement is expected in the coming days, according to the Huffington Post.

It came after Mr Burnham said last month that out-of-town warehouses, which act as deployment centres for the likes of Amazon and Asos, should face higher business rates to lower the levy on hospitality venues.

He has also reiterated his pledge to expand small-business rates relief for small firms from 50pc to 100pc.

Mr Burnham said: “I believe there is a case for higher business rates on warehouses and the major developments we see on the outskirts of our cities, so we can cut business rates for pubs – I proposed a 20pc cut – and lift some high street businesses out of business rates altogether.”

Calculations by Ryan, the global tax firm, show that cutting business rates for pubs, clubs and live music venues by 20pc would cost £133m.

On top of a larger rates overhaul to support small high-street firms, which could run to around £880m a year, the policy would cost more than £1bn.

‘Higher costs for the consumer’

Raising business rates on warehouses and out-of-town developments has previously been suggested by Mr Burnham as a way to fund the tax break for pubs.

According to Ryan, Mr Burnham could change the formula used to calculate business rates to hit warehouses with higher tax bills.

The UK Warehousing Association, the trade body for the sector, wrote to Mr Burnham on Monday warning him against imposing higher business rates on warehouses and major out-of-town premises.

Clare Bottle, the chief executive of the trade body, said: “Business Rates have become grossly complex, prone to unpredictable swings and now act as a major inhibitor to investment.

“The Prime Minister’s proposal would make a bad situation even worse. Endless tweaking of thresholds and reliefs, supposedly paid for by warehouse of ‘online giants’, will only mean more money for valuation consultants and higher costs for the consumer.”

Ms Bottle added that changes to business rates under Sir Keir Starmer had been “particularly punishing”.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) warned against increasing business rates on warehouses, saying that it would impact high street stores who receive goods from distribution centres.

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the BRC, said: “Retail pays the largest share of business rates in the country, levied on every part of the industry, from high street and retail park stores, to warehouses and distribution centres.

“Funding rates reform by adding even more taxes to an already overburdened industry will ultimately mean struggling families foot the bill through higher prices.”

She added that business rates are a major force pushing up costs paid for by customers.

The Treasury was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heat pump households face winter rationing threat
data.description:
Households with heat pumps face having their central heating turned down remotely over winter in a push to avoid the risk of blackouts.

Licenced electricity suppliers will be able to control heat pumps via the internet by the end of next year under Ed Miliband’s plans to help Britain’s energy system cope with high demand.

The proposed Smart Secure Electricity Systems scheme would allow grid bosses to tell the likes of Octopus Energy and British Gas to ration heat pump power when the electricity network comes under strain.

This would typically be during winter cold snaps.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) said the rationing system, officially referred to as “load control”, would help balance the grid during peak demand, making blackouts less likely.

The Government said energy suppliers “will be able to remotely control significant amounts of electrical load through EV [electric vehicle] charge points, heat pumps, etc”.

It is proposed that only households that agree to become part of the system would be included. In return they would be offered discount tariffs.

Blackout risk

The risk of blackouts is expected to become more extreme as net zero policies shift energy demand from gas to electricity and supply becomes more dependent on wind and solar generation, which are less reliable than fossil fuels.

More than 223,000 heat pumps are installed across the country, according to the latest official statistics. The Energy Secretary has set a target for 450,000 to be installed annually by 2030. Since April last year, every new heat pump must be able to connect to the internet.

Ministers and officials are seeking ways to crimp electricity demand as signs of strain on the grid become more apparent.

Whistleblowers at the National Energy System Operator, DESNZ’s quango in charge of the grid, have claimed that bosses sought to cover up just how close the country came to blackouts during June’s heatwave.

An independent investigation into the claims – which were made public by Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary – has been launched.

‘Labour has no permission for this’

Addressing the heat pump plans, Sir Iain Duncan Smith, the former Conservative leader, said: “The Government says the purpose is to balance the electricity grid but it’s like living in a totalitarian state.

“The Government has no permission for this, its energy policy has become an ideology.”

Opponents warned that remote control of heat pumps could lead to dangers during cold snaps or heatwaves as more households adopt the technology.

Kathryn Porter, an energy consultant at Watt-Logic, said: “These proposals are particularly worrying in the context of recent hand-wringing over air conditioning: providing remote control over heat pumps would allow third parties to prevent cooling altogether while potentially restricting heating on cold winter days.”

Ms Porter said the Government’s plans “have people serving the grid rather than the other way round”.

Experts called on Labour to guarantee that under the system, heat pump households would be able to override remote orders.

There are also concerns about the risk of heat pumps being hacked once connected to the internet.

A DESNZ spokesman said: “Smart heat pumps from reputable manufacturers already have robust security protocols in place, so the cybersecurity risk is low.

“We have consulted on new rules which go even further, so every heat pump sold in the UK meets the strongest security standards.”

The proposals are under consultation as Mr Miliband prepares for a potential move away from energy policy to another senior Cabinet position under Andy Burnham.

The effect of such a move on Britain’s net zero policy is unclear.

Mr Burnham has signalled that he will support North Sea oil and gas drilling, which Mr Miliband has stymied.

data.title: Billionaire brothers sue Scottish government over subsidies for Chinese buses
data.description:
The billionaire brothers behind a major bus operator are suing the Scottish government after it earmarked more than £20m in subsidies for Chinese buses.

McGill’s, owned by Sandy Easdale and James Easdale, applied for a £4.3m grant to acquire 33 electric buses from the Scottish manufacturer Alexander Dennis (ADL), but was denied.

The Scottish National Party government reached the decision despite bankrolling a furlough scheme at ADL last year to save 400 local jobs after the firm said it might move to Yorkshire.

McGill’s argues that the Chinese grants, awarded in March as part of a £45m push to decarbonise Scotland’s buses, breached subsidy rules and that the decision should be overturned.

It has filed a claim with the Competition Appeal Tribunal in what it described as a multi-million-pound legal action.

The firm, which is Britain’s biggest independent bus operator, claims that no subsidy control assessment was carried out and that funds are being used to support new routes rather than eliminate diesel fleets.

It said it would challenge Scottish ministers and Transport Scotland about how the competing applications were scored and seek to force a “comprehensive rethink” of how zero-emission subsidies are allocated.

Sandy Easdale said McGill’s investment plan would have protected skilled jobs at Falkirk-based ADL while removing diesel vehicles from its nationwide fleet.

Instead, he said, the company was denied funding, while the largest handout – around £13m – was awarded to Edinburgh-based start-up Ember for the purchase of 100 coaches from Yutong, the world’s largest bus manufacturer, based in Zhengzhou, China.

Mr Easdale said the move would add to Ember’s all-electric fleet while doing nothing to take existing diesel buses off the road.

Transport Scotland says in its own guidance on the latest awards from the Scottish Zero Emission Bus Challenge Fund that the funded project should “reduce greenhouse gas emissions and improve local air quality”.

Mr Easdale said: “We have questionable decisions being made on large amounts of public money by nameless people in secret rooms who are trying to avoid scrutiny.

“Decisions on taxpayers’ sums of this magnitude should be fully transparent, and decision-makers should be willing to be held accountable.”

Ember’s handout will fund 100 buses and 43 charging points. Two other recipients were also granted money for purchases from Yutong, with First Group receiving £6m and Stagecoach being handed £4.6m to fund a total of 66 Chinese buses and coaches.

Two further firms, Rock Road and Lothian, received £11.5m and £5.6m, respectively, for the acquisition of 153 buses, 30 of them from Wrightbus of Northern Ireland and 123 from ADL.

Transport Scotland declined to comment on ongoing legal proceedings. It said the Scottish government provided £8.7m to McGill’s for 41 buses purchased in an earlier phase of the funding scheme.

McGill’s was also part of a consortium awarded £41.7m for 42 buses and received funding through the Scottish Ultra-Low Emission Zone Bus scheme for 68 vehicles.

data.title: Sport will only get more politicised, warns top agent after Trump World Cup
data.description:
Sport must “carefully monitor” growing political incursions, the head of one of the world’s largest sports marketing agencies has warned.

Adam Kelly, the British president of IMG, predicted that “governments are going to get increasingly involved” as other forms of entertainment fragment online and politicians latch on to sport’s enduring power to draw mass audiences.

The 48-year-old, who has run the business for almost seven years, said: “There is a growing dynamic there that needs to be carefully monitored.

“Sport is playing an increasing role in society as other dynamics fall away. And if that is true, then you’ve got to think, well, governments are going to get increasingly involved.”

His comments come as what has been labelled the most politicised World Cup in history draws to a conclusion.

Among other spectacles, it has seen Donald Trump successfully lobby Fifa to suspend a US player’s red card ban, a referee caught up in Trump’s immigration clampdown, Argentine players lay false claim to the Falkland Islands on the pitch and Iran take on Egypt in a group match designated as the official gay pride fixture. (IMG’s hospitality sister company On Location meanwhile provided the pitchside sit-down meals that raised eyebrows among European fans.)

IMG is part of TKO, a $35bn (£26bn) New York-listed giant which also owns World Wrestling Entertainment and the Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC). It is enjoying a marquee year thanks in part to the sporting calendar. IMG and On Location reported revenue growth of 38pc in its most recent quarter to $655m, propelled by demand for luxury hospitality at the winter Olympics.

TKO this month staged UFC Freedom 250 to celebrate the semiquincentennial of the United States for unmistakably Trumpian tastes.

The $60m mixed martial arts event demanded the construction of a 4,300-seater arena on the White House lawn, alongside motocross stunt ramps. Mark Shapiro, the TKO chief, nevertheless proclaimed “this isn’t about politics”.

Regardless, according to attendee Kelly – speaking during Wimbledon fortnight in more genteel surroundings a short walk from the All England Club – the apparent public thirst for connection via sport inevitably means a more political business.

A Wimbledon local but an Arsenal fan, he said: “When we won the league, 200,000 people went to the Emirates Stadium, even though we weren’t playing there that evening.

“There was this almost religious experience that people shared that was beyond anything they had experienced before.

“That’s their church, synagogue, mosque. People chose to congregate. There’s a natural instinct of people to want to do that. As other things fall away in society, does sport fill that space? If that’s true, then the governments will get more involved.”

Andy Burnham seems certain to. He was one of the authors of a 2020 report that became a foundational document in the creation of the new football regulator. The incoming prime minister was reportedly keen on more radical action, too, such as mandated fan ownership.

This era of politics and legalism is all a long way from the spirit of amateurism that is still detectable at the All England, where IMG has handled some of the unseemly business of the Championships’ commercial operations – such as selling the television rights – for more than 50 years.

The Wimbledon relationship has been an island of stability for IMG in a storm of change in recent years, as Gulf state and private equity money has poured into the business of sport.

The weather is now transforming again, as the Iran conflict prompts a strategic and fiscal rethink for the Arab autocrats and especially Saudi Arabia. Via its Public Investment Fund, it had become global sport’s favourite few-questions-asked bankroll. Decisions such as the withdrawal of funding from LIV Golf have already brought a welcome shot of rigour, according to Kelly.

He said: “In sport, not all problems are solved with capital and certainly not capital that comes in the form of equity.

“I implore rights holders in sports to see that the currency is not in chasing the money, it’s chasing the fan.”

LIV’s “major thing wrong”, for instance, was that it “didn’t follow through with finding an audience in the right way and then building fans, not just of golf, but fans of LIV”.

Some sporting bodies “who don’t know what questions to ask and don’t know the consequences” have taken sovereign and private equity cash to the detriment of fans in pursuit of a “short-term win”, Kelly added.

Rugby’s entanglement with the private equity firm CVC is often held up in the industry as an example of a mismatch in expectations and culture that has failed to deliver for the sport.

CVC’s stakes in various rugby competitions have recently been rolled up with interests in European football leagues and the women’s tennis tour under a new umbrella, Global Sport Group, with highly rated management brought in. The structure has nevertheless been forced to take on heavy debt in a refinancing in lieu of the onward sales of equity originally envisaged.

Kelly said: “If the sports they have invested in had simply raised that debt themselves, it would be at a far cheaper cost of capital than selling equity to CVC to then refinance with debt.

“Certainly the main people who have benefited from those transactions are CVC and their partners. And outside a couple of examples, I find it hard to pinpoint the benefit to the sports.”

This ongoing reality check is healthy, Kelly said: “There’s a tightening of the rationale, not necessarily the tightening of capital. I think that is far better for the long term. I know of sports bodies that have taken capital, wish they hadn’t and now are able to assess what they should do next and in a much more educated manner.”

The other end of IMG’s world, the entertainment companies who buy television rights and collect the money from consumers which should fund the whole circus, is also facing major upheaval. The decline of traditional television is bringing mergers of hitherto independent customers – such as Sky and ITV in the UK – and shifting the tectonics of price negotiations.

The correlated arrival of Netflix (boxing), Apple (Formula 1), Amazon (football) and YouTube (NFL) as buyers should be more than enough to offset any impact, Kelly predicted. IMG’s media rights business has flatlined so far this year, however.

He said: “It’s happening at the right pace and I think it is going to accelerate significantly over the next few years.

“Sport drives attention and the attention economy is one of the oldest economies in history. If you’ve got a premium live audience that you can predict, you understand, you can effectively convert into earnings, that is a highly valuable asset. That is the fundamental.

“Netflix, Amazon, Apple, Google are better than any organisation in history in that economic conversion. As they come in, the value of sport will increase.”
