data.title: Currys’ turnaround king tasked with reviving tired Boots ahead of flotation
data.description:
When Currys announced that Alex Baldock, its chief executive, was stepping down earlier this year, the response from investors was immediate.

Shares in the electronics chain fell sharply – wiping almost 11pc off its valuation – as markets digested the departure of the man widely credited with engineering the company’s quietly successful turnaround.

Now Baldock appears poised for an even bigger challenge, tasked with reviving a 177-year-old brand that has seen better days. On Wednesday, it emerged that the outgoing Currys chief was being lined up to take the reins at Boots.

The company confirmed his appointment on Thursday, setting the stage for Baldock to try to convince investors that the British chemist could be worth as much as £7bn.

Baldock should be well-suited to the role. He has spent the past seven years proving that legacy retailers can still thrive if they modernise quickly enough.

At Currys, that meant slimming down the store estate, cutting costs, boosting online sales and emphasising its “omnichannel” offering of services, repairs, credit and subscriptions.

Adam Tomlinson, a retail analyst at Berenberg, says that when Baldock arrived at Currys in 2018, “the business was in a very tough place”.

“A major focus initially was restructuring the mobile division, which had become a significant drag on the wider group. He completely realigned it in terms of right-sizing both the store base and the cost base, and turned it from being a problem area into a positive contributor.”

Tomlinson says Baldock’s broader strategy has been to transform Currys from a “product-first retailer into an integrated services business”, encouraging customer loyalty through credit offers, delivery and installation, warranties, repairs and trade-ins.

“What is often overlooked is how much focus he placed on staff and colleagues,” he adds. “He has a mantra that customer experience can never exceed colleague experience.”

Baldock will now have to repeat that success at Boots – a brand which is desperately in need of a turnaround.

Since being folded into Walgreens Boots Alliance in 2014, Boots has endured closures, restructurings and repeated attempts to move away from its old-fashioned chemist image.

Its owners have repeatedly signalled that they would be keen to offload the British business. Walgreens launched a sale process for Boots in 2022.

However, it later scrapped the plans after failing to secure a deal at the valuation it wanted in the face of difficult market conditions and rising borrowing costs. Since then, efforts have been focused on honing the chain, including pushing through further store closures and cost reductions.

Speculation over a sell-off of the British arm was revived last year when private equity firm Sycamore Partners swooped to buy Walgreens Boots Alliance in a $10bn (£7.4bn) deal.

Shortly after the deal was completed, Sycamore split Walgreens into five standalone entities – one of which was Boots. It was seen as the first major step towards ultimately returning the British chemist to the UK stock market.

Baldock is believed by many to have been brought on to get Boots over the line for such a London listing – something insiders claim was “always the plan”. Its owners are looking for a valuation as high as £7bn. Still, some remain unconvinced that any listing would be imminent.

A senior retail industry source says his appointment is the same “as with any private equity-owned business”. “The job is to create as much value as possible for the owners,” the source says.

Given the current state of the London market, they say it is more likely that Baldock has simply been brought in to put Boots on a stronger footing. “I don’t think it’s right that he’s been brought in for an IPO.”

They say Boots’ health and beauty division is now “by far the most important part of the business”, reflecting the growing view inside retail that the future of Boots lies less in pharmacy and more in cosmetics, skincare and wellness.

That shift is already visible inside many of Boots’ larger stores, where beauty halls increasingly dominate the shop floor while traditional pharmacy space shrinks.

Boots has revamped more than 100 of its stores and updated beauty halls in more than 180 sites. It argues that this has helped it to record 20 consecutive quarters of market-share growth.

Analysts expect Baldock to continue pushing the business in the same direction because beauty products offer significantly stronger margins than dispensing medicines or selling low-cost healthcare essentials.

Jonathan De Mello, of JDM Retail, says Boots is “looking at devoting more floor space to beauty rather than distress pharmaceutical purchasing. The margins on beauty products are significantly better”.

At the same time, Boots still faces the challenge of balancing investment in physical stores with the need to boost online sales.

For all the investment Boots has poured into flagship stores and digital infrastructure, analysts argue the business still lags behind rivals on both fronts.

“The Advantage Card is strong, but the website still needs work,” says De Mello.

Former executives claim there are also still swathes of its sprawling estate which are in urgent need of investment. Renovating all of these old stores would be painfully expensive.

Clive Black, another retail analyst, agrees the chain’s estate remains uneven despite years of investment.

“There are some flagship stores where they have clearly invested heavily in beauty, but there are also an awful lot of stores around the country that look pretty tired,” says Black.

If Baldock wants to take Boots public – be it later this year or further down the line – he will have to convince investors that Boots can be a growth business rather than simply a reliable cash generator.

Black says he suspects that “Boots may end up being seen more as an income story than a pure growth story”.

Baldock will also inherit questions over Boots’ international operations.

While the retailer’s presence outside the UK may have formed part of the attraction for him personally – having steered Currys for years in both the UK and overseas – analysts suggest some international businesses could ultimately be sold off as management focuses more tightly on the chain’s strongest markets.

De Mello says Boots’ operations in places such as Germany and Mexico are underperforming. He argues the business would be better concentrating on the UK and Ireland, which he describes as the company’s “engine room for growth”.

At Currys, Baldock managed to convince the markets that physical stores still had value in the Amazon era, provided they worked seamlessly alongside a strong online offer. Between 2024 and 2026, he oversaw a tripling in the retailer’s share price.

Just last week, Baldock told The Times that he loved his role at Currys. His next move would have to be “something properly exciting to take me away from Currys”. With Boots, it seems that Baldock has found that. Now, he said, marked the start of “an exciting new chapter for Boots”.

data.title: Safety warning over Labour’s rail nationalisation plans
data.description:
Labour’s plans to nationalise the railways threaten to undermine passenger safety, the industry watchdog has warned.

The Office of Rail and Road (ORR) said the upheaval of bringing train operators and Network Rail under state control meant senior railway executives could lose focus and start to ignore safety issues.

Great British Railways (GBR), the new state-backed operator, is taking back rail line franchises as they come up for renewal, with remaining routes expected to come under government control by the end of next year.

However, Richard Hines, the chief inspector of railways, said in the ORR’s annual report on health and safety that the disruption of nationalisation could bring short-term risks.

“GBR is an opportunity to strengthen safety across the network but change must not weaken protections or blur who is responsible for keeping passengers and workers safe,” he said.

“As rail reform progresses, there must be clear leadership, clear accountability and a relentless focus on known safety issues, such as over-speeding.”

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said the warnings from Mr Hines were “concerning” and urged Labour to review its timetable for nationalisation.

He said the findings added to concerns about the impact of the GBR plan, which the Conservatives have claimed will drive up costs and leave passengers and taxpayers to pick up the bill.

“The chief inspector of railways has warned that Labour’s reforms risk weakening accountability for rail safety,” he told The Telegraph.

“Safety must always come first. If the evidence shows that Labour’s rushed nationalisation is compromising the safety of passengers and railway staff, it should be paused until ministers can guarantee those risks have been addressed.”

In its report, the ORR also warned that work to tackle deaths on level crossings, worker fatalities and over-speeding at junctions – recent cases of which had come close to “catastrophic outcomes” – should not take a back seat to the creation of GBR.

The regulator said the crash near Bedford last month – which killed a driver and sent 100 passengers to hospital after a stationary train was hit by another travelling at 50mph – had provided a “sobering reminder” of the severe consequences of rail accidents.

The ORR said that while progress had been made in some areas, “the management of risk is not yet delivering the overall level of control and assurance required”.

The regulator singled out over-speeding, in which trains travel faster than the permitted limit for a given section of track, as one of the most significant safety issues.

Mr Hines also said the safety of maintenance staff remains a key concern after a spate of deaths, including one in March when a track worker was killed after being hit by a train at 110mph.

Fatalities on level crossings are also increasing, he said, while the derailment of an express train following a landslide at Shap, Cumbria, indicated the increased importance of “robust infrastructure management in the face of environmental pressures”.

He said the causes of the Bedford collision remain under investigation.

Initial findings suggested the driver who was killed passed a red signal after the train in front came to an unexpected halt when a fault triggered its emergency braking system.

A Department for Transport spokesman said: “Safety on our railways is our number one priority. 

“We have been working with industry and independent experts, including the ORR, to make sure safety is at the heart of everything we do as we set up Great British Railways.

“Our rail reform programme will not change the robust rules in place on safety and as we move forward, we will build on the decades of experience which have meant Britain’s railways consistently rank among the safest in the world.”

data.title: Ryanair crew failed to help passenger almost sucked out of window
data.description:
Ryanair crew failed to help a passenger when he was nearly sucked through a window after it shattered mid-flight, it has been claimed.

Ljubiša Karović, 61, from Serbia, fainted several times after he was dragged into the hole during a flight from Thessaloniki, Greece, to an airport near Munich on July 9.

His wife, Svetlana Grković, who was seated three rows behind, rushed to help as the window smashed and oxygen masks dropped from the ceiling of the cabin.

Ryanair insisted its cabin crew had done a “phenomenal job” in responding to the incident involving Mr Karović, before telling The Telegraph that it was conducting a formal investigation.

However, Ms Grković said it was fellow passengers – and not cabin crew – that saved her husband.

She told the Guardian: “I rushed over too. None of the cabin crew helped. It was the passengers. One man, I think he was an Albanian, was incredible.

“He did all he could to bring him in and then tried to block the window, first with a bag that was immediately sucked out and then with a suitcase, which worked. We are eternally grateful to him.”

It is thought Mr Karović was “half in and half out of the plane” for up to two minutes.

He said: “They tell me I fainted twice, and again inside the plane, and that my face was briefly swollen and deformed because of the pressure, the rush of air. My wife reminds me about that.”

Vasilis Tsiaras, Mr Karović’s lawyer, said his client “dipped in and out of death”.

The lawyer added: “When the cabin lost pressure he was hurled out of the window from his chest up and immediately lost consciousness. He was on that plane heading to Germany to celebrate his brother’s birthday.

“Instead, at 15,000ft he ended up with his head and right arm outside the aircraft as it moved at a speed of more than 600km/h. He looked at death, that other world, and came back.”

Mr Tsiaras said: “We have no complaint with the pilot, who followed the safety protocols and dealt with the situation very well.

“My client fainted two or three times, once outside the aircraft when he was partially sucked out of the window, and twice inside when, again, he lost consciousness. At no point did the cabin crew offer to help.

“It was the other passengers who rallied and gave Ljubisa water.”

He added that the airline had not even contacted Mr Karović or his wife to offer any assistance. But it had contacted their son, who booked the flights, about rebooking.

In a statement to the Guardian, the airline said: “As soon as the aircraft descended to a safe altitude our cabin crew attended to and assisted Mr Karović, moving him to alternate seats (beside his wife) and arranged for a doctor onboard to sit with them until the aircraft landed safely back in Thessaloniki.

“They also arranged to have medical assistance standing by on landing in Thessaloniki.”

Mr Karović was discharged from hospital this week and is wearing a neck brace.

Doctors have told him he will have to wear it for six weeks, after which they will decide if surgery is needed.

Mr Karović said he should be offered compensation rather than “emails that have focused on rebooking the flight”.

And he urged other passengers to always wear their seatbelt, adding: “I am lucky. My head and neck still hurt, but I am alive. It could have been the seatbelt, the fact that I was still strapped in. But maybe it is fate. I believe in God and I thank him every day.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The humans training their AI replacements
data.description:
In April, the race to develop and roll out truly autonomous Artificial Intelligence (AI) seemed to enter a disturbing new phase when viral videos surfaced showing human workers wearing headsets featuring cameras that were tracking their movements.

We’ve become accustomed to AI systems scraping vast amounts of data from the internet to train, improve and update themselves. But, as demand for increasingly sophisticated robots that can operate in the physical world grows, human workers are being co-opted to train their own replacements.

In India’s garment manufacturing industry, a firm called Egolab AI supplied manufacturer Pearl Global Industries with headsets for its staff to wear. As the workers were stitching, folding, handling cloth and making the tiny adjustments to the items they worked on, a machine watched, hoovering up training data.

According to reports, workers had been told the devices would record what they did during their shifts, but had not been asked for written or verbal consent.

The principle is called capturing “egocentric video footage”, which can be used as training data for humanoid robots. It brings us closer to the industrial ideal of so-called “dark” or “lights-out” factories, which can run with very few humans (or none at all), because the machines have replaced workers in every part of the manufacturing process.

In north-east China, carmaker Zeekr is one example of this kind of lights-out manufacturing. Vehicles roll off a heavily automated line without a human hand touching them. The International Federation of Robotics said China installed 295,000 industrial robots in 2024, 54 per cent of the global total, showing how quickly the country is trying to automate its manufacturing base.

Closer to home, one of the world’s biggest companies is apparently trying to do something similar for office work. In late April, Meta, the owner of Facebook, Instagram and WhatsApp, announced internally that it was testing a tool called the Model Capability Initiative (MCI), which would run on US-based company devices and capture mouse movements, clicks and keyboard inputs – as well as occasional screenshots – to help build so-called “agentic” AI systems that can work without oversight.

“This is where all Meta employees can help our models get better simply by doing their daily work,” the internal announcement claimed. The programme has not yet been implemented in the UK or Europe because of stronger workplace protections and data-protection rules.

But, even with that geographically limited rollout, the response within Meta was hugely negative. “It’s totally digital surveillance. It’s mining your work and your data to find a way to replace you,” says one former employee who was made redundant from the company in May along with 8,000 or so others, accounting for around 10 per cent of the total Meta workforce.

“A lot of people internally were very upset about it, and were speaking up about how this is the wrong choice,” adds the ex member of staff, who wishes to remain anonymous. In fact, more than 1,500 Meta employees signed a petition against the MCI, saying that “collecting and repurposing this kind of data raises serious concerns around privacy, consent, and trust in the workplace.”

The petition also alleged that the initiative was “intrusive, coercive, non-consensual data collection”. And, addressing Mark Zuckerberg and Meta leadership directly, added: “It should not be the norm that companies of any size are permitted to exploit their employees by non-consensually extracting their data for the purposes of AI training. AI meant to serve and empower people cannot do so while disregarding or disrespecting the consent of the people it claims to serve.”

Asking staff to grant permission to a tool to track their every digital movement in order to help better train AI systems that are in turn designed to ultimately replace them was seen as beyond the pale for most employees.

“It feels gross to be monitored like that,” the former employee says. “How would you feel in the workplace if your every keystroke, and every movement, was tracked and recorded to create this product?

“Let’s be real. They want to create a product that they can sell to others that says, ‘You can make your human employees dispensable.’”

Speaking to Reuters after the plans were first revealed, a Meta spokesman emphasised that the system was not being used to monitor the performance of staff, but said: “If we’re building agents to help people complete everyday tasks using computers, our models need real examples of how people ​actually use them – things like mouse movements, clicking buttons, and navigating drop-down menus.”

Meta paused the programme at the end of June after learning that unauthorised individuals within the company were able to access the data collected.

Despite that, experts have warned this is the direction of travel within many industries. That doesn’t bode well for innovation. As the former Meta employee explains: “People have asked me, do I actually think that AI can replace the work that I do, and the honest truth is, no. This is a technology that, like many technologies, can accelerate certain repetitive tasks, but fundamentally – and I worked on these internal systems – it’s a probabilistic model. It doesn’t do critical thinking.”

As Catherine Flick, an AI ethicist at the University of Staffordshire, puts it: “These sorts of systems are trained on existing data, and so therefore they’re just a mirror on things that have already happened.”

The backlash to Meta’s initiative created another, unforeseen, problem for the company: as well as the petition, signed by more than 1,500 staff, some employees boasted on business social networks that they were deliberately making errors in their work, or working in an intentionally lackadaisical manner, in order to feed the AI model bad training data.

Whether such actions could meaningfully impact the model’s capabilities is yet to be seen. But the decision by a Silicon Valley giant to roll out such a programme is itself a sign of a new workplace norm in the AI era. Where Meta leads, others are likely to follow.

“This is the message that I want to share,” says the ex-employee. “Sooner rather than later, it’ll not just be Meta employees or tech workers affected by this, but anyone who is a knowledge worker.” They call it a “wake-up moment”.

And even if, like the ex-staff member, an individual thinks AI will never be able to do their job as well as they can, that may not be the view of their boss. “This is a trend that is really concerning for your own livelihood,” adds the ex-employee. “I think a lot of skilled knowledge workers, especially in tech, but in other places, think that, somehow, they have skills that are special and not replaceable.”

Flick adds that it is also “a really egregious thing for companies to do”.

But, says the ex-employee, bosses have already shown they do not value human labour in manufacturing industries and other sectors. “Why would you think that that’s not [going to be] the case for engineers or writers or data scientists?” Ultimately, the ex-staff member says, “we’re all dispensable to the handful of billionaires” who control the new systems.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour chaos leaves Rolls-Royce’s engine plans up in the air
data.description:
As political turbulence buffets No 10, the expansion plans of one of Britain’s biggest manufacturing businesses are at growing risk of being blown off course.

Rolls-Royce, the £120bn aerospace giant, fears that Labour’s leadership turmoil will ground its £3bn bid to build engines for the best-selling short-haul passenger jets made by Boeing and Airbus.

The engineering giant had been close to securing vital development funding for the scheme after intensifying discussions with Sir Keir Starmer’s Government, The Telegraph understands.

But a change of prime minister is expected to result in a spending reset and there is no guarantee that Andy Burnham will sign off on the financial support should he become Labour leader.

After a 15-year absence, Rolls plans to re-enter the £1.2tn short-haul engine market under the leadership of boss Tufan Erginbilgiç, dubbed “Turbo” for his success in reviving the fortunes of the FTSE 100 business.

The former BP executive, who described Rolls-Royce as a “burning platform” on his arrival in 2023, has ended losses, halted a slide in market share and overseen a 15-fold increase in its stock price.

Lift-off for Rolls as it returns to the market for single-aisle planes – the mainstay of fleets at carriers such as Ryanair and easyJet – would allow it to compete in a sector that accounts for 60pc of global jet sales.

Erginbilgiç has said the plan to build engines for a new generation of aircraft being developed for the mid-2030s, though costly, would represent a vital phase in the company’s revival, creating 40,000 jobs and swelling UK exports.

Sources said that while funding talks have progressed beyond support for research and development to include the full industrialisation of the programme, capital requirements are such that Rolls is reluctant to commit to the plan without the support of Downing Street.

Rob Watson, the company’s president of civil aerospace, said last week that a reevaluation of UK spending priorities seemed plausible, despite “good ongoing discussions.”

He said: “We are always talking to them about options to have government support for programmes we want to introduce.

“Every government wants to make sure they scrutinise and understand the plans. If there’s a change in government, maybe we’d expect them to review the plans.”

Speaking in a briefing at the firm’s manufacturing centre in Derby, Watson said Rolls could still fall back on its core business of making engines for larger wide-body planes, if the conditions for re-entering the short-haul business were not right.

“Narrow-body is a really good growth market that we haven’t been in for 10 to 15 years,” he said. “So when we consider options for growth, it’s naturally one we look at.

“But this group has a great growth trajectory with our core businesses today. We are not compelled to do anything.”

A lack of backing from the next prime minister could push Rolls into the arms of Germany, where its Dahlewitz plant is set to produce the complicated gearbox mechanism for its next-generation Ultrafan turbine, on which the new narrow-body engine would be based.

German officials have mounted a charm offensive to secure further work, and Watson confirmed that those contacts continue.

Rolls is also understood to be concerned that the political upheaval could provide an opening for General Electric (GE) to pitch its own rival proposals for the next generation of short-haul engines. Previously, it had been losing ground to Rolls’s Ultrafan blueprint.

The US business and Safran of France, partners in a venture called CFM that supplies engines for current Boeing 737s and Airbus A320s, have proposed a radical design change that dispenses with a traditional engine casing to draw in far larger quantities of air.

By accelerating a massive amount of air gently rather than a small amount more violently, CFM reckons that it can create more efficient thrust and save up to 20pc on fuel.

However, the so-called “open rotor” appears to the eye like a throwback to propeller planes, featuring two sets of swept back and heavily twisted fan blades poking out into open air. Some at Rolls have nicknamed it “the angry egg-whisk”.

While Airbus is keen on the technology, Boeing is believed to be far from convinced.

Airlines also remain cautious amid concerns about noise levels, trickier repairs and the reaction of passengers.

Problems with the current generation of conventional jet engines, which pushed current know-how to the limit only to suffer constant breakdowns and recalls, have also reduced their appetite for exotic new designs.

Rolls believes the Ultrafan would deliver fuel savings without resorting to unproven technology by using an internal gearing system to boost efficiency.

However, one insider said there were concerns that any hiatus in its engine proposals would be to the benefit of the Franco-American CFM venture, which could even seek UK funding by promising future maintenance jobs.

Rolls is continuing to explore options for a manufacturing partnership that could help it bear the cost of the project, Watson said.

A revival of a former alliance with Pratt & Whitney, another GE rival, is seen as the most probable outcome, though the pair would need to find a way to combine their efforts. Watson said Rolls was “thinking very creatively” about alternative avenues.

Meanwhile, the company appears to be almost out of the woods with fixes to the Trent 1000 engines that power Boeing 787 long-haul planes. These have suffered issues with premature wear for more than a decade.

The glitches have led to the grounding of hundreds of jets for emergency repairs. Carriers including British Airways and Virgin Atlantic blamed Rolls for disruption to flights.

Willie Walsh, the head of the International Air Transport Association, went so far as to claim that Rolls’s share price gains were “not right” given the faults. He said last month that engine-makers should “stop gouging” airlines “and get back to making engines that work”.

Rolls’s Watson declined to comment directly on the remarks, saying he was “very happy that our customers set exacting standards”, but noted following a £1bn upgrade programme Rolls had outperformed in delivering some of the improvements it had promised disgruntled carriers.

Celine Bouas, the firm’s senior vice president for customers, admitted that the issues with the Dreamliner engine had inevitably “caused pain” to airlines.

“Customers lose revenue and profitability and if it happens in the middle of the summer it’s very painful,” she said.

However, she said that more than a third of the Trent 1000s currently flying have now undergone modifications to turbine blades that have tripled the interval between maintenance visits. The entire fleet is set to be upgraded by the end of next year.

Bouas added that the improved reliability was central to South American airline Latam selecting Rolls engines for its latest batch of 787s, despite it having earlier defected to GE.

Another deal from an airline that has used rival GE and CFM engines “for decades” is also close, she said, leading Rolls to believe that it can regain between 40pc and 60pc of the market.

In this area at least, “Turbo” Tufan’s turnaround appears to be gathering pace. But for now, Downing Street’s political tumult mean Rolls’s engine plans are still up in the air.

data.title: Labour nationalises British Steel
data.description:
British Steel has been nationalised by Labour as ministers vowed to protect the future of steel production in the UK.

The Government confirmed on Thursday it had taken the Scunthorpe steelworks into public ownership, seizing formal control of the business from China’s Jingye.

Ministers took over the operations of British Steel in April 2025 from ‌Jingye in order ⁠to stop its blast furnaces ⁠from being ​shut down and to protect 2,700 ​jobs at ‌the plant and ​thousands of roles ⁠in its supply chain.

On Wednesday, Parliament passed a bill that allowed ministers to take formal ownership of the plant in the public interest.

Sir Keir Starmer said: “Today’s decision secures the future of steel-making in the UK, protects skilled jobs and safeguards a vital national capability.”

The Government took over the plant despite it already costing taxpayers an estimated £1.3m per day, according to the National Audit Office.

Andrew Griffith, the shadow business secretary, accused Labour of “channelling 1970s nationalisation vibes”.

He said: “You can’t fix steel without ditching Labour’s ruinously high energy costs.

“A blank cheque from the taxpayer isn’t the answer.”

While it lost operational control in 2025, Jingye had retained formal ownership of British Steel, meaning the Government has been legally unable to sell all or part of British Steel, or make decisions about its strategy.

The Chinese company has been seeking compensation of up to £1bn for the loss of the steel facility. It rejected an offer of £100m in February.

Last month, the Beijing-based business triggered a formal process to recover money it claims to have invested in the Scunthorpe steelworks before its operations were seized by the Government.

The Government seized control of the management of British Steel in 2025 amid concerns that Jingye was planning on walking away from the steelworks.

There were fears that if its furnaces had been shut off, they could never have been reignited, leaving Britain unable to produce its own high-quality steel.

The site is seen as crucial national infrastructure, despite years of decline for the British steel industry. It is home to Britain’s last two operating blast furnaces and produces 95pc of the steel used on UK rail tracks.

Jingye had previously said the business was losing £700,000 a day amid soaring energy costs. However, ministers have said that they could limit or refuse compensation.

The Government previously stopped short of a full nationalisation and had sought a commercial buyer to take control of the plant.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, told Sky News on Thursday that it would not necessarily get cheaper to produce steel.

“Can it become more profitable and can we have more productive means of producing steel? Then I believe yes,” he added.

“If this plant were to close, if British Steel were to disappear, we would become dependent on global suppliers ... Yes, it is an extensive investment, but just think about the alternative.”

British Steel’s Scunthorpe plant has had a difficult history, with a number of failed attempts in the past to turn it into a profitable business.

Greybull Capital, a British private equity group, bought the business from Tata Steel in 2016 for £1, only for it to collapse into administration in 2019.

Jingye took over the plant in March 2020 by buying British Steel from the UK’s official receiver for about £50m.

The Chinese business had promised to spend £1.2bn upgrading the plant’s blast furnaces with net-zero-friendly technology.

However, the British site had struggled to compete with the flood of cheap steel coming from China.

data.title: The Yorkshire town facing a housing invasion from over the border
data.description:
When Ingrid McLaughlin, 75, and her husband moved to Wetherby more than a decade ago, it was the perfect place to call home.

A market town of around 12,000 people on the edge of West Yorkshire, it is right in the centre of Yorkshire’s so-called “Golden Triangle” between Harrogate, York and Leeds, where the scenic settings, excellent schools and top-tier amenities mean house prices remain consistently strong.

Yet 11 years on, McLaughlin now fears the worst for Wetherby.

Her town faces a wave of speculative development, with thousands of homes planned under the banner of the ‘Wetherby Urban Expansion’.

Crucially, these proposals are not driven by Leeds city council, the local authority that governs the town. Instead, private developers are targeting land just over the North Yorkshire border – an unwelcome expansion that residents fear will hollow out the very essence of Wetherby to meet housing targets many deem undeliverable.

Locals, however, have little power to fight back. Under changes to the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) in December 2024, the Government watered down protections for communities to challenge developments, while also increasing housing allocations to meet Labour’s pledge to build 1.5 million new homes over the course of the parliament.

As a result, North Yorkshire council’s target more than tripled overnight, from around 1,300 per year to over 4,000.

Residents now say the additional housing demands mean developers are placing some of the burden on its West Yorkshire neighbour.

Proposals for nearly 1,000 homes across several developments on the North Yorkshire side are currently being considered. Alan Lamb, a Conservative councillor for Wetherby ward, estimates that some 5,000 more homes could potentially be built through a patchwork of smaller developments.

“If this carries on, Wetherby could end up with the equivalent of a whole new small town bolted on to it, swallowing up nearby villages, losing large areas of productive farmland, and putting huge pressure on roads, schools and health services,” he says.

Although technically in the neighbouring county, these estates would be physically and socially tied to Wetherby – without paying council tax towards any additional infrastructure in the area.

Despite Wetherby having already met its own housing allocations set by Leeds city council until at least 2042, it now faces the prospect of an unwelcome expansion that locals fear would fundamentally alter the community and its charm.

“The developers are talking about these new homes as if they are part of Wetherby in the planning applications, and we know, of course, it’s all happening in North Yorkshire,” McLaughlin says.

“The council tax will go to North Yorkshire, and we will be left with very serious infrastructural problems afterwards.

“Quite apart from the traffic congestion caused by all the extra vehicles that will inevitably clog the roads, people will be coming into Wetherby for doctors, dentists, the schools, and other services – yet we’re already bursting at the seams.”

Under Labour’s new planning framework, ministers can overrule councils on major developments of more than 150 homes.

Many areas protected by Green Belt status are also at risk of being downgraded to Grey Belt, opening up places for development that were previously restricted to prevent urban sprawl.

Penny Stables, a Green councillor for Wetherby ward, believes the changes to the NPPF have left communities vulnerable.

“Proposals for thousands of new homes just north of the town would overwhelm local infrastructure and permanently damage the landscape,” she says.

“Britain does need more homes, but they must be the right homes, in sustainable locations, with infrastructure in place first. Instead, developers continue to prioritise the most profitable schemes on greenfield sites.

“The problem is not local planning committees. It is the growing disconnect between what developers want to build and what communities actually need.”

‘Transport is a serious concern’

With no railway station and infrequent bus services, locals fear the influx of new homes will push already stretched roads to breaking point.

“Transport is a serious concern,” says Brian Taylor, the acting chair of residents’ group Better Wetherby.

“We’ve got this historical town with narrow roads built for the horse and cart a few hundred years ago, and now we have SUVs and heavy goods vehicles going through the town centre.

“They’re building these car-dependent estates on the outskirts, and so we’re worried about the extra burden on the highways, and the problems associated with it such as air pollution, noise pollution, and parking.”

Les Tyler, a member of Better Wetherby, adds: “Wetherby’s a lovely place to be, and we all love it. We accept that it’s going to develop, things are going to change – of course they are – but it’s a question of making sure that what’s done is planned.”

Many people feel it is now developers and ministers in Whitehall who retain the ultimate power over the future of Britain’s towns and villages – with residents forced to live with the consequences.

And communities like Wetherby can do even less when the planning applications affecting their town fall outside the boundary.

“People in Wetherby are very much at the behest of somebody else with these decisions, since those powers have been taken away from us,” Connor Mulhall, the town’s mayor, says.

“It’s the speed that the developments are starting to be proposed as well, and I can envision them getting a lot quicker as authorities try to push them through.”

Diminished local powers could give rise to an increase in developments on the boundaries of other administrative authorities.

Similar examples of councils using boundary-edge developments in Leicestershire and Staffordshire have already been raised in Parliament by MPs speaking on behalf of their constituents’ concerns.

As Britain battles its housing crisis, those opposed to North Yorkshire council’s boundary-edge developments insist their objection is not Nimbyism.

“Wetherby isn’t saying ‘not here’,” Lamb says. “We’re already doing our bit. We’ve planned for new homes properly, and those homes are being built.

“The problem is that the Government has changed the rules overnight, creating a free-for-all that is completely undermining communities that have planned responsibly.”

North Yorkshire council said it was unable to comment on live planning applications when approached for comment.

“It’s funny because I can see some people’s eyes rolling, but I just think if someone doesn’t say it, nothing will change,” McLaughlin adds.

“I’ll just have to keep banging on about the same things, and I will continue to, because I’ve got grandchildren, and I think it’s dreadful what they’re going to inherit.”

data.title: UAE sells £4.4bn Vodafone stake to French telecoms tycoon
data.description:
The United Arab Emirates has sold its stake in Vodafone to a French telecoms tycoon, ending an investment in the company that triggered national security concerns.

Abu Dhabi-based E& has struck a £4.4bn deal to sell its stake of more than 16pc to Xavier Niel, who will become the company’s largest shareholder.

Mr Niel said the investment was intended to be a “long-term, strategic minority shareholding” and that he had no intention of making a full takeover offer.

The deal, which was carried out through Mr Niel’s newly formed family holding group Vega, will bring to an end a partnership struck between Vodafone and the UAE’s state telecoms operator in 2023.

Hatem Dowidar, E&’s chief executive who was handed a seat on Vodafone’s board, has stepped down with immediate effect. E& will make a net cash return of £970m from the sale.

The UAE’s stake in Vodafone has long been the source of controversy, with ministers declaring it a national security risk in 2024.

Oliver Dowden, deputy prime minister at the time, forced the telecoms giant to establish a special committee to oversee any work with a bearing on British security.

Ministers warned that Vodafone, which holds sensitive Whitehall contracts and owns critical infrastructure such as undersea cables, risked “material influence” by the Gulf state.

E& said the decision to sell its stake in Vodafone reflected a “natural evolution” of priorities to “sharpen its strategic focus on core businesses”, while freeing up cash.

Mr Niel, who owns French mobile and internet giant Iliad, has previously sought to woo Vodafone by bidding €10.5bn to buy its Italian operations, though his approach was ultimately rebuffed.

He has long had ties to the telecoms giant, taking a 2.5pc stake in the business in 2022 through Atlas Investissement.

The French telecom tycoon owns the telecoms brand Free and is also a major investor in newspaper Le Monde.

Mr Niel said: “Vodafone is a compelling investment opportunity, underpinned by quality assets, strong brands, leadership positions and a diversified geographic footprint.

“As a simpler, more focused business, Vodafone is ready for a new phase of growth and is well-placed to unlock substantial untapped value across its European and African operations.

“We are confident Vodafone can deliver sustainable growth and strong cash flow generation over the long term and – as an anchor investor based in Europe – we are ready to contribute our deep sector expertise and operational know-how to its future success.”

Vodafone said: “We know the Niel family group well and look forward to engaging with them as a supportive, long-term shareholder.

“They recognise the quality of our diversified operations and have confidence in the new chapter of Vodafone’s growth.”

Shares in Vodafone jumped as much as 13pc following the announcement, catapulting it to the top of the FTSE 100.

The stake sale follows a turnaround plan implemented by Vodafone’s boss, Margherita Della Valle, who has sought to simplify the sprawling telecoms group and boost its ailing share price.

Under her tenure, Vodafone has sold off operations in Italy and Spain and carried out a £15bn deal to combine the network operator with Three in the UK.

The company struck a £4.3bn deal in May to buy out Hong Kong billionaire Li Ka-shing’s 49pc stake in VodafoneThree. The move will give it full control of the venture.

data.title: Britons poorer than Americans ‘because of City red tape’
data.description:
Britons are 40pc poorer than Americans because City red tape has held back economic growth in the UK since the 2008 global financial crash, new research claims.

The Institute for Economic Affairs (IEA) said that rules introduced after the crisis made it harder and more costly for banks to lend to private households and businesses in Britain.

The think tank said in a briefing note that red tape has held back UK economic growth to a greater extent because small and medium-sized businesses rely more heavily on banks for financing compared with the US.

It added that, unlike in Britain, small American banks were governed by fewer regulations in the wake of the financial crisis.

This helped lending to non-financial businesses in the US recover to pre-financial-crisis levels in mid-2013. However, in the UK bank lending is still 15pc lower than in early 2008.

Tyler Goodspeed, the chief economist at ExxonMobil and a White House adviser to Donald Trump during his first term as president, said regulators in Britain had “with the best of intentions, made it structurally harder for banks to lend.

“That was a choice, and it is still being made today.”

Before the global financial crash, economic growth was slightly higher in the UK than the US.

However, while the US endured a sharp fall in economic growth in 2008 before recovering, the UK has been unable to boost growth to its pre-crash level.

This stagnation in economic growth in the UK over the last 18 years has left Britons 40pc poorer than Americans.

Lord Hannan of Kingsclere, the director general of the IEA, said: “Britain has underlying structural problems of its own creation that undermine its ability to grow and create wealth and prosperity, including banking regulation. We made it too hard for banks to lend to businesses.”

It comes after the Bank of England’s financial policy committee announced earlier this month it was easing rules for UK lenders implemented after the 2008 financial crisis as part of a summer deregulation drive.

The committee said it would lower the leverage ratio, which determines a bank’s overall ability to lend.

In a speech at the annual Mansion House dinner last week, Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, said: “There is a lot of commentary that regulation is having an excessive negative effect on growth. We shouldn’t ignore this.”

However, Mr Bailey argued against a wholesale push to cut red tape, insisting that “well-designed regulation should support economic growth, not hinder it”.

He added: “To simply ​argue for less regulation is unhelpfully reductive.”

The Treasury and the Bank of England were contacted for comment.

A Prudential Regulation Authority spokesman said: “The 2008 crisis resulted in a deep economic recession which significantly reduced the wellbeing of the people of the UK. Returning to the pre-financial crash regulations would be a mistake.”

data.title: ‘It brings people right to our door’: what it’s like living on a famous footpath
data.description:
When the King’s 2,700-mile England Coast Path was in planning, only a tiny fraction of the 25,000 landowners consulted about the project raised objections.

Many talked about potential rewards. “Camping, caravanning, bed and breakfasts, cream teas, you name it,” said Neil Constable, of Natural England.

Honor Scott’s family can attest to the value of living near a well-known footpath.

Her great-great-grandfather established Matson Ground, a mixed upland farm in the heart of the Lake District National Park, in 1923. Securing certified organic status in 2000, today it covers 500 hectares of farmland and woodland above Windermere.

In 2019, the family converted Birkdale House, an original Lake District villa on the farm, into a holiday let that sleeps 10 and boasts views of Lake Windermere and the fells. Not only that, but the Dales Way, a 79-mile footpath connecting Ilkley in West Yorkshire to Bowness-on-Windermere in Cumbria, lies just 100m from the house.

While Scott, 29, accepts that there are occasional problems with walkers, most are well-intentioned. “The footpaths literally bring people to the door, which is not only great for the holiday lets but also for our business as we’re hoping to start selling our meat direct to customers.

“If they’ve seen the way the animals live on the farm while walking through, what better way to market our products?”

Alongside visitors, Scott points out that lots of locals regularly use the footpath, too.

“It’s so nice for us as we get to know the people who live here better and they have a connection to the farm.

“In June this year, we did a pop-up burger stand using farm produce at the Lake District Book Festival for the first time and ended up feeding lots of those who walk through the fields and past our cattle, which was great.”

A mixed blessing

However, living on or near a public footpath can be a mixed blessing. For those who run businesses such as campsites and cafés, a steady stream of walkers can provide valuable passing trade.

But for homeowners, the same access can bring a loss of privacy and may affect a property’s value. And because public rights of way are legally protected, re-routing a path can be a difficult, lengthy process.

When someone is trying to sell a country property, the presence of a footpath can create tension. Back in 2007, when a retired entrepreneur, who did not want to give his name, bought his house in East Suffolk, the conveyancing lawyer failed to spot that there was one footpath running up the side of the property and another going through some marshy ponds in the garden.

“In reality, there was already a de facto diversion in place, so no one had used either path for years,” he said.

But recently, when preparing for the house to go on the market, the two footpaths appeared in the local searches conducted by the estate agent. The owners were advised that their presence could take £50,000 off the property’s value or cause a buyer to get cold feet entirely.

The owners approached the council to have the footpaths extinguished from the register, a process that involved a series of consultations with the parish council, the Ramblers (formerly the Ramblers’ Association), and members of the public.

The request was granted, provided the couple installed a gate and paid £4,500 in fees. In all, it took about a year to go through.

The fact that no one uses the paths makes the process easier, but well-established footpaths are notoriously hard to move. Precedent plays a significant role, especially for unmarked paths, if there is evidence they have been used frequently for more than 20 years.

Cheffins, a firm of surveyors, has recently had success with a beef-farming client based in East Anglia. The 800-acre family-run farm had a popular footpath running through the middle of the busy farmyard, which they wanted to have diverted.

While there were no formal objections to the plans and the new route was only marginally longer than the previous one, the process took five years and cost the family approximately £5,000 in fees. “In my 30-year-long career, I’ve only worked on a couple of these projects. They aren’t for the faint-hearted,” says Simon Gooderham, a chartered surveyor.

Buyer beware

Many of those who dream of owning a house along the 422 miles of Cornwall’s coastline will have to contend with the South West Coast Path in some way.

When Harlyn House, a historic 15th-century Cornish mansion near Padstow, was put on the open market for £3m in 2017, Clare Coode, of Stacks Property Search, viewed it on behalf of clients, but no one was willing to take the plunge.

“The problem was that a footpath goes across the drive and they weren’t prepared to take that on. In the end, it was turned into The Pig at Harlyn. It’s the perfect solution: as a hotel, the footpath presents no problems.”

There are safety responsibilities that all landowners take on when a footpath crosses their property. Permanent or temporary blockages, such as locked gates or an overgrown hedge, are unlawful. If the highway authority is forced to intervene, the landowner can ultimately be billed for the work, explains Matthew Scott of estate agent Strutt & Parker.

The quirks of owning a property on or near a coastal path go further, says Coode. “It’s not just about where the path is now but where it might be in the future – thanks to coastal erosion. If it gets worse, the path which goes in front of the house may need to be rerouted around the back – not everyone has bargained for that.”

Screening is an option, but it is an expensive one, and not much apart from gorse grows happily when being bashed by Atlantic winds, and it can mean a view becomes obscured.

Footpaths can have a greater effect on home values away from the coastal path, where the loss of privacy is felt more keenly.

The thatch-and-honeystone hamlets, plunging valleys, and pretty beech woods that make up the Cotswolds National Landscape have long attracted walkers clutching copies of OS maps in waterproof cases. The area has one of the highest concentrations of footpaths in England.

Gemma Maclaran, of buying agent Prime Purchase, estimates that a footpath running close to a house or through the garden could potentially take 20pc off the value.

For some people, however, ramblers popping up in their garden do not bother them.

The 186-mile Pembrokeshire Coast Path runs just a few metres from the house of an IT consultant, who did not want to give her name. She has very few quibbles about having people walking past her front door.

“I just gave directions to a group of eight walkers and a dog so they could avoid a field full of cows. Coastal path walkers are generally lovely,” says the 53-year-old.

In Scotland, the Land Reform Act 2003 has granted the public the “right to roam” since its introduction. This means that people have the freedom to walk, cycle, ride, and wild camp on most land and inland waters across the country, provided they do so responsibly.

In the last few years, those campaigning for a Scottish-style model to be rolled out all over the UK say momentum has grown. To date, however, the Government has not committed to adopting such a model, making a wholesale transplantation of Scotland’s system unlikely for now.

So those living on footpaths remain in a unique position, whether they like it or not.

data.title: Paramount accused of ‘blackmail’ with threat to leave Hollywood
data.description:
Paramount has been accused of blackmailing California by threatening to leave the state if officials there try to block the company’s takeover of Warner Bros.

The Hollywood studio giant has reportedly been considering moving its headquarters away from Los Angeles and shifting as much as $30bn in spending out of the state in response to legal action.

Advisers are said to be urging David Ellison, Paramount’s chief, to consider quitting Hollywood and moving elsewhere, according to US website Semafor.

However, Rob Bonta, California’s attorney general, told industry magazine Deadline: “It seemed like a last-ditch effort to try to blackmail my office.”

It came as Mr Bonta confirmed that California was leading a dozen US states in legal action aimed at blocking Paramount’s mega-merger with Warner Bros.

The lawsuit, filed in Northern California’s district court, warned that Paramount’s planned takeover of Warner Bros would inflict “substantial harm” on cinemas, cable TV operators and audiences.

The $110bn (£82bn) Hollywood merger has already been approved by the US Justice Department but the coalition of states is seeking to block the deal from closing until its lawsuit concludes.

The intervention poses a major threat to Paramount’s attempts to redraw Hollywood by combining two of its biggest film studios. The company is controlled by Oracle tycoon Larry Ellison and led by his son, David.

In an apparent barb at the Ellison family’s ties to the Trump administration, Mr Bonta said: “In this country, no one is above the law.

“With this lawsuit, California and our sister states are fighting for free and fair markets, not rigged markets. America has no kings in government or our economy.”

Paramount is facing bitter public opposition to the deal, with critics arguing that it will lead to fewer films being released in cinemas and less choice for audiences.

More than 200 academics, documentary makers and TV journalists have signed an open letter warning that the proposed takeover would lead to “improper political meddling” and have “severe consequences for American democracy”.

Opposition has been centred on the fact that Mr Ellison will gain control of news network CNN following the deal. He already owns CBS News after striking an $8bn deal to take control of Paramount last year.

‘Sweeping changes’ to appease Trump

Critics accused the Ellisons of making “sweeping changes” at CBS to appease Mr Trump, including pulling segments critical of the US president’s policies. The president has described Larry Ellison as a “good friend”.

Any decision to shift Paramount’s corporate headquarters out of California would be seismic for the US entertainment industry, as it would be the first of the major film studios to do so.

However, it would come in the wake of an exodus of jobs and production from Hollywood in recent years spurred on by writers’ strikes and as rival hubs offer generous tax breaks.

Paramount would also not be the first major company to ditch California. Mr Ellison’s Oracle relocated to Texas in 2020, while Elon Musk’s Tesla followed suit the next year.

The Ellisons have committed to keeping both the Paramount and Warner Bros studio lots in California open following the merger.

In the lawsuit, the states said the combined company would control 27pc of the film distribution market in the US, rising to 30pc for big-budget blockbusters. It would also hold 27pc of the market for cable TV channels.

The states, which also include Arizona, Massachusetts, New York and Washington, said the tie-up would lead to higher prices, the decline of cinema viewing and a reduction in the variety, quality, and amount of content distributed.

The legal action could rack up hundreds of millions of dollars in extra costs for Paramount. The terms of the Warner Bros takeover include a so-called ticking fee under which the company has agreed to pay an additional $650m for every quarter it is delayed beyond Sept 30.

The company has previously warned that delays could force it to renegotiate the deal’s financing – which includes $24bn from three Gulf states – potentially putting the transaction at risk.

The states have asked Paramount and Warner Bros not to complete the deal until the legal process is complete. If the companies fail to do so, the states threatened to file a temporary restraining order.

A Paramount spokesman said the lawsuit “reflects a fundamentally flawed application of the antitrust laws and is wrong on both the facts and the law”.

They added: “We will vigorously defend the transaction and demonstrate that this challenge is inconsistent with sound competition policy and the competitive realities of the media marketplace.”

data.title: UK pension giants ‘most exposed in Europe’ to shadow banking risks
data.description:
UK pension funds are the most exposed in Europe to risky shadow banking activities, a new report warns.

Fund managers who underwrite British retirement schemes have twice as much money tied up in the sector than their European counterparts, according to S&P Global.

Legal & General (L&G), Standard Life and Just Group, for example, have more than 10pc of their funds invested in illiquid private credit versus 5pc for funds in Europe, the report said.

UK pension insurers have 8.8pc allocated to private credit, with European insurers allocating 4pc and European re-insurers just 1pc.

The high levels of exposure will raise fears about risks lurking in the UK pension system.

Shadow banking has faced mounting scrutiny after investors suffered significant losses following the collapses of US firms Tricolor and First Brands, which had both taken on large private credit loans.

The collapse of Market Financial Solutions in February similarly sent shock waves through the City, as banks, including Barclays and HSBC, recorded hundreds of millions of private credit losses from the property firm’s sudden failure.

Andrew Bailey, the governor of the Bank of England, also warned in May that pension funds had become dangerously intertwined with the private credit market.

City funds such as L&G and Standard Life have grown significantly in the last few years by acquiring corporate pension schemes from some of the country’s largest employers, in so-called “bulk purchase annuity” deals.

The biggest private credit investors in the world have also moved into the sector, with Just Group and PIC recently acquired by Brookfield and Apollo respectively.

S&P said the recent acquisitions “have fuelled concerns that private-capital-owned insurers will increase allocations to private capital, as is already the case in the US”.

Pension funds have found themselves drawn to private credit investments as it typically offers significantly higher returns to investors who are willing to lock away investments over an extended period.

However, such investments are viewed as more risky because of a widespread lack of transparency in the private credit market and difficulties in turning them into cash because of the limited pool of buyers.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Grid capacity squeezed as net zero projects delayed
data.description:
The UK grid operator has been forced to scramble for extra power supplies after delays to many of the UK’s most crucial net zero schemes.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso), a quango which runs the UK power system, has had to buy in at least two gigawatts (GW) of extra gas-fired generation capacity to ensure it can keep the lights on for the next five years.

The revelation comes in a report from experts appointed to assess Neso’s plans as it tries to hit the Government’s target of decarbonising the grid by 2030.

The report, published by the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, warned that many planned low-carbon generation projects were being delayed or cancelled, so the UK would need extra gas-fired backup.

It said: “There is an upward trend in non-delivery ... which has averaged 1.8 GW over the last five years and 2.1 GW over the last three years.”

The lost capacity equates to several of the UK’s nuclear power plants, of which five remain, each with a capacity of just more than one gigawatt. Four are due to close by 2028.

The report warned that paying for extra generation capacity now was important to ensure the UK’s lights could stay on.

It said: “It is prudent to account for more non-delivery at the T-4 stage [four years in advance], as Neso has proposed this year ... This buffer would allow decarbonisation with less risk to security of supply.”

The UK has more than 30 gas-fired power plants with an increasing proportion supplying the capacity market, switching on when low-carbon sources such as nuclear, wind and solar cannot meet demand.

In theory, they can supply a total of 38 GW of capacity – but about half of the plants are more than 25 years old and so increasingly unreliable without expensive upgrades.

Delayed new low-carbon generation projects include the Hinkley Point C Nuclear Power Station, under construction in Somerset.

Originally expected to come online in 2025 at an estimated cost of £18bn, the completion date has slipped to after 2030 with overall costs escalated to more than £50bn.

Soaring inflation in the offshore wind sector has also caused key projects to be cancelled altogether, including the massive 2.4 GW Hornsea 4 offshore wind farm.

Danish developer Ørsted warned that supply chain costs had made the scheme unviable.

The report warned that more cancellations were probable: “It is widely recognised that the costs of most new-build power stations have increased significantly in the last few years, as evidenced in the reasons for the cancellation of a number of new projects.”

Separately, a 2025 analysis by Onward, a think tank, found that delays in constructing vital high-voltage grid transmission lines linking new offshore wind farms in East Anglia and the South East to demand centres had led to bottlenecks of clean power.

Connection problems have plagued gas-fired power station projects too, as recently happened with some open-cycle gas-turbine plants commissioned by Drax. They should have been running since autumn 2024 but delays in grid connections mean the first – in Hirwaun, Wales – has only just been commissioned.

Kathryn Porter, from Watt-Logic, an energy consultancy, said: “The current four year construction timeframe does not take account of real world delivery schedules and supply chain constraints.”

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said the procurement of extra generating capacity was aimed at maintaining the security of the UK’s electricity supplies and showed the capacity market was working as intended.

A spokesman said: “Our capacity market ensures security of supply while providing value for money for consumers. We’ve cut VAT on electricity to give families breathing space and our focus is working to bring bills down for good.”

Neso was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: China attacks Labour over British Steel nationalisation
data.description:
Beijing has attacked Labour’s decision to nationalise British Steel, warning it will damage Chinese investment in the UK.

The Government confirmed on Thursday that it had taken the Scunthorpe steelworks into public ownership, seizing formal control of the business from China’s Jingye. Labour said the move would protect the future of steel production in Britain.

China’s commerce ministry (Mofcom) said it “firmly opposes and was strongly dissatisfied with the UK Government’s decision”.

Ministers took operational control over British Steel in April 2025 in order ⁠to stop its blast furnaces ⁠from being shut down and to protect 2,700 jobs at ‌the plant and thousands of roles ⁠in its supply chain.

A Mofcom spokesman said the move would deal a “severe blow to Chinese companies’ confidence in investing in the UK”.

“China will closely follow developments, support Chinese companies in safeguarding their rights through legal means, and take strong measures to firmly protect the interests of Chinese companies,” they added.

The decision has strained relations between Britain and China just as Andy Burnham prepares to enter No 10.

The Department for Business and Trade said nationalising British Steel was essential to maintain steel production at the company’s site in Scunthorpe.

Bringing it under public ownership for the first time since 1988 was also vital to protect both the company’s future and UK supply chains, ministers said.

Jingye demanded compensation from Britain in June for losses from investments made before the Government took over operation of the plant.

A Mofcom spokesman added that the UK should “earnestly fulfil” its obligations under the China-UK bilateral investment treaty.

The Government took over the plant despite it already costing taxpayers an estimated £1.3m per day, according to the National Audit Office.

Mr Burnham, who will become Labour leader on Friday and prime minister on Monday, is expected to widen public ownership in industries such as water and energy.

One of his first tests will be what to do with heavily indebted Thames Water. Mr Burnham could bring the company into special administration, a form of temporary nationalisation.

Sir Keir Starmer said on Thursday: “British Steel is part of the fabric of our nation and a cornerstone of Britain’s industrial strength.

“Today’s decision secures the future of steelmaking in the UK, protects skilled jobs and safeguards a vital national capability.”

The Department for Business and Trade has been contacted for comment.

data.title: The great Netflix turn-off
data.description:
Music fans will be familiar with the curse of the difficult second album, when an act they like follows up a wonderful debut record with a disappointing one (think Second Coming by the Stones Roses or U2’s October). Football supporters are well versed in discussing second season syndrome, the phenomenon where a newly promoted side far exceeds expectations in its first Premier League season before dropping away in the next campaign (Birmingham City in 2011 and Sheffield United in 2021 spring to mind).

Netflix bosses may be forgiven for worrying about second series syndrome. The entertainment world is abuzz with chatter about how recent follow-ups to some of the streaming giant’s most popular shows have struggled to get anywhere near as many viewers as the original outings.

Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, was asked about the trend when unveiling the company’s financial results on Thursday, and attempted to prove the doubters wrong. “It’s very common in the industry, but it’s even more so with us because we launch our shows so big,” he said. “Our global reach, our discovery mechanism, releasing all at once… This enables us to find a very large audience early, so our shows tend to start really big while most other places, their shows start pretty small and occasionally grow from there.” Sarandos added: “Our season-two fall-off is actually slightly improved this year relative to last year.”

Despite meeting Wall Street’s financial expectations, the Netflix share price slid after it reported its numbers; the market value is down 18 per cent this year and more than 50 per cent over the past 12 months, wiping billions off its worth. Investors, at the very least, are worried about Netflix’s problems. As for viewers, they’re left with the growing sense that the world’s number-one streaming service is running out of shows worth watching.

A brief glance at Netflix’s own viewing data – it publishes the top-10 most-watched films and TV shows weekly – illustrates what has been described as a growing source of concern. The second series of The Four Seasons, Tina Fey’s couples comedy series, had fewer than half the viewers of its debut in its first four weeks (32.9 million vs 14.5 million). The Night Agent, the FBI thriller series, has gradually dropped from racking up 73.9 million views in the first four weeks of its first series, to 40.9 million views of its second, to 26.7 million of its third.

The Four Seasons

The Night Agent

At time of writing, the second series of Avatar: The Last Airbender has only been available for three weeks. But while the first series, one of Netflix’s most watched in 2024, had 50.2 million views in that time, its follow-up only pulled in 23.5 million. The first series of Wednesday, the Jenna Ortega Addams Family smash hit that is the all-time most-watched show on Netflix, had 252.1 million views in the three months after its 2022 launch; the second garnered 119.3 million views in the same time period after it was released in two batches last year.

Avatar: The Last Airbender

Wednesday

There are outliers, of course. The fourth and fifth series of Stranger Things are third and fourth, respectively, on Netflix’s all-time list of popular English language series and ahead of the show’s first instalments; Bridgerton has continued to add more viewers after series two.

So why is this happening? Since Bloomberg published a story about this phenomenon earlier this month, many explanations have been offered without being definitive. Some have suggested that the long gaps between series is behind the soft numbers for follow-ups, but the drop-off is as real for the second instalment of The Sandman, Neil Gaiman’s fantasy show that had three years between instalments, as Ted Danson’s A Man on the Inside, the first series of which topped the weekly Netflix chart on debut but didn’t make it on to the top 10 list for the second one year on.

The Sandman

Others reckon that Netflix’s typical release model – which allows viewers to binge-watch series rather than building interest with weekly episodes – is to blame. Shows such as HBO’s The White Lotus and The Pitt have grown their audiences by keeping viewers hooked and evolved into word-of-mouth hits by becoming what is known in the industry jargon as appointment-to-view.

And then there is the perception held by some that the quality of Netflix’s output just isn’t up to much – that it is hard to find much that is actually worth watching. “Most Netflix shows are not great”, Vulture, the entertainment news site, quoted an executive at one of Netflix’s rivals as saying this week. “So when these not-great shows go away for a year and a half or two and then come back, nobody cares. Nobody’s built a lasting relationship with them, and so they move on.” Other series, such as The Boroughs, were reasonably popular but got cancelled after just one outing.

It is something of a puzzler. “You can’t just go: ‘Why this is happening is because X.’ It’s a bunch of things,” Tom Harrington, head of TV at research outfit Enders Analysis, tells me. “Some of the shows might just be bad. Some of it might just be they came out at a time when there was another, better show on somewhere else, or [elsewhere] on Netflix.”

And to be fair to Netflix, this is not just an issue with which it contends. All of its rivals have to deal with what Harrington calls the “natural attrition” of falling viewer numbers as a series progresses. “Every TV show, bar a few, loses viewers – that’s the gravity of TV,” he says.

Then there are the structural issues that the entire mainstream entertainment industry is dealing with: namely, the continued growth of YouTube and social media dragging eyeballs away from more traditional films and series. “It’s a lot harder for everybody in terms of long-form viewing, because a lot of it’s been taken away by short-form viewing,” says Harrington. “And it’s going to naturally become more difficult to hold on to viewers.”

Netflix appears to be at a crossroads after more than a decade of relentless success and growth – remember, the streamer only started showing its own original series when it launched House of Cards in 2013 – in which it has become the world’s undisputed number-one entertainment company. It surprised many when it agreed a deal to buy the streaming and studio assets of Warner Bros Discovery, which would have given it a huge library to populate its streaming service, only to be gazumped by Paramount.

In some ways, Netflix is a victim of its own success and its commitment to transparency. It is the only one of the big streamers or broadcasters to publish weekly viewership data that can be turned into a stick with which to beat it, while it also makes a point of releasing engagement reports every six months that go into great detail about its most popular series and films.

Perhaps as an implicit acknowledgement that its bosses are uncomfortable with the scrutiny that comes with being the world’s pre-eminent entertainment outlet, Netflix will now only publish the massive engagement report once a year from January. “Netflix is running a familiar play when a metric gets uncomfortable: it shifts the spotlight,” wrote Mike Proulx, at Forrester Research. “Pulling back engagement reporting at the exact moment engagement is in the spotlight gives off a strong ‘nothing to see here’ vibe.”

It is not totally clear from where salvation will come. Recent releases such as Little House on the Prairie and The Hawk have not exactly been a hit with critics. Summer highlights include the Lockerbie drama The Bombing of Pan Am 103, an animated series from Ricky Gervais and… not much else.

For the rest of the year, Netflix is pinning its hopes on returning series such as Black Doves, the festive Keira Knightley spy thriller that is expected in the winter, and Guy Ritchie’s The Gentlemen in September to boost viewers. Debuting series include a new Pride and Prejudice starring Emma Corrin, Jack Lowden and Olivia Colman.

And it is branching out into areas that its bosses had previously suggested it would rather avoid. So it is that Netflix is increasingly getting into live sport, launching games, putting podcasters Gary Lineker and Jay Shetty on screen and striking deals to put videos from publishers BuzzFeed and Condé Nast on the service.

These pivots are “out of character”, according to Harrington. “They used to be very focused and very disciplined around ‘This is what we are, this is what we do, we don’t make acquisitions, we don’t have ads, we don’t do sport, we have a very clean product offering.’ And then things get a little bit harder, and things slow down, and then they have to break some of the rules that they have.”

Is it an identity crisis? “I think that they’ll probably say, ‘Well, we’re trying to expand our offering rather than not knowing who we are, and some things will work and some things won’t work,’” Harrington adds. “I guess they’ll lean into what works. They can’t just live off House of Cards for ever.”

Netflix shows certainly feel less important, less all-consuming, in our continually fragmenting culture. The time when the world seemed united in its obsession with, say, The Crown feels like an age ago. The best way for the streamer to quieten its sceptics is to deliver some new smash-hit productions. That is easier said than done, of course.
