data.title: Mansion tax sparks £247k plunge in Westminster house prices
data.description:
Labour’s upcoming mansion tax has triggered the biggest fall in Westminster house prices on record.

Average house prices in the central London borough fell 22.8pc from £1,083,519 in May 2025 to £836,331 in May 2026 – the fastest decline since Land Registry data began in 1996.

The looming mansion tax and an increase in mortgage rates since the outbreak of the Iran war prompted the decline.

In the broader London market, prices dropped 3.7pc to an average of £554,814 in the year to May, which was the fastest fall in house prices for two years.

The capital was the only region in the UK to record a decline in annual property prices in that time, the data showed.

Labour’s mansion tax, known as the high-value property surcharge, will hit homes worth more than £2m with a tax surcharge of at least £2,500 a year when it comes into force in April 2028.

House prices in the capital have also been hit by the scrapping of non-dom status and higher stamp duty rates.

Lucian Cook, the head of residential research at Savills, said: “I think there is an element of caution around the tax environment. They already know that they’re exposed to the high-value council tax surcharge.”

He added: “There’s also a bit of a legacy effect in terms of some of the changes which have been made to non-dom taxation.”

In Hammersmith and Fulham, property prices fell 10.9pc in the year to May, the fastest annual decline since July 2009 when the property market was still reeling from the global financial crisis.

House prices in Kensington and Chelsea declined 11pc year on year to an average of £1.26m in May 2026, down from £1.4m in the same month a year earlier.

Richard Donnell, the executive director at Zoopla, said: “There has been a lot of talk of wealth taxes, taxing higher value properties … If you’re an overseas buyer or someone thinking about relocating, London and the UK have had a lot of uncertainty around.”

He added that inner London property prices “are effectively resetting”.

Homes in London are poised to bear the brunt of the mansion tax because of the high concentration of expensive homes in the region.

Mr Cook said speculation about further tax rises in the coming months was likely to weigh on higher-value markets as John Healey, the new Chancellor, prepares his first Budget.

“Westminster and Kensington and Chelsea are typically prime central London markets, which are much more exposed to some of the political uncertainty, but equally the tax environment,” he said.

In recent weeks there have also been rumours that Andy Burnham may lower the mansion tax threshold to £1.5m, as the new Prime Minister searches for ways to fund new policies.

Expanding the tax would drag an additional 137,000 homes across England into its scope, according to analysis by estate agency Hamptons, bringing the total number of properties hit by the tax more than 271,000.

A government spokesman said: “This tax is expected to raise around £430m per year to help fund public services and is addressing a longstanding unfairness in our country, where a Band D home in Darlington or Blackpool pays more in council tax than a £10m mansion in Mayfair.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Germans contemplate working Sundays to save their economy
data.description:
“Sunday is the great day on the Continent. The free day, the happy day,” Mark Twain, the American author, wrote in 1880, after visiting the German spa town of Baden-Baden.

Almost 150 years later, these words no longer ring so true. Germany’s venerated “Sonntagsruhe” or “Sunday rest” has become an economic battleground.

Friedrich Merz, the chancellor of Germany, is looking to pull every lever at his disposal to kickstart Germany’s flagging economy, taking aim at everything from tax and red tape to welfare spending and sick leave.

Retailers are pushing to extend this reform blitz to the country’s century-old prohibition on Sunday trading, which once a week turns German high streets into ghost towns.

“Germany needs reforms to help its economy. So every stone is getting turned over, or should be. And we’re saying, ‘Don’t forget our stone,’” said Timo Weber, the boss of department store chain KaDeWe.

They face an uphill battle. Churches, trade unions and politicians from across the political spectrum stand in their way. And they’re fighting a tradition that goes back a long, long way.

In 1919, “Sonntagsruhe” was enshrined in the new Weimar Republic constitution, which said Sundays and public holidays should “remain protected by law as days of rest from work, and of spiritual improvement”. This has carried over into Germany’s current constitution.

Across most of the rest of Europe, even in relatively religious countries, shops and supermarkets can trade on Sundays, at least to some extent.

But in Germany only cafes, restaurants and petrol stations can open, unless there’s a festival on. It is still as quiet as Twain found it in the 19th century.

The difference from Twain’s time is that people are no longer so attached to “Sonntagsruhe”. In separate polls by INSA and YouGov this month, only about half of those surveyed still backed the ban.

‘The German illusion’

Merz has leaned into this gradually opening door. His reform package will allow bakeries and confectioners to open for eight hours on a Sunday, rather than just three. Public libraries’ allowance will increase from three hours to six.

This has kicked off renewed debate over whether the “Sonntagsruhe” is a beneficial tradition or a painful anachronism.

Churches and social groups argue that Sunday closing offers a respite from commerce and promotes social cohesion.

Retailers say that these days people shop online on Sundays anyway, so trade is taking place regardless.

These orders are often being fulfilled from Poland, says Nils Busch-Petersen, the head of the Berlin-Brandenburg Retail Association.

“There are thousands and thousands of employees directly behind the Polish border, working every Sunday to keep the German illusion of a free Sunday,” he says.

Retailers also argue that Sunday shopping has become a leisure activity, so it should be on the same footing as other ways people might spend their day off.

According to Weber, when KaDeWe is occasionally allowed to open on Sunday, its biggest rivals are “the Italian restaurant where you’re going with your family or the stadium you’re going to for a football match”.

“It’s the museum, it’s the cinema, it’s streaming, it’s online shopping, it’s just staying at home. These are our real competitors in terms of time and experience, and we’re not given equal treatment.”

The trade unions are opposed. They warn that relaxing Sunday trading will put employees at risk of being forced to work unsociable hours.

Retailers counter that Germany’s labour laws, which are stronger than Britain’s, ensure that staff working on a Sunday are paid extra and earn time in lieu.

Weber says that on the 10 Sundays a year when his Berlin department store can open, he has more staff volunteers than he needs.

Smaller businesses are less enthusiastic.

‘Put it in the hands of the retailers’

“We have trouble finding employees who are willing to work every Saturday, so how could we find employees for a Sunday?” says Catrin Schulz, a Berlin optician.

“Also, none of our customers have ever asked for it. We wouldn’t have any benefit if we kept our shop open.”

Busch-Petersen says nobody is suggesting shops should be forced to open. He characterises Sunday trading as a three-way pact between the business owner, the employees and the staff.

“If just one of that trio doesn’t want it, there will be no open store. But if all three want it, why shouldn’t they come together on Sunday?” he says.

For him, this is emblematic of a much wider issue. “The question is whether you need to forbid opening to those who want it. It’s a basic societal question: does the state regulate everything or do you let these things regulate themselves?”

Weber agrees: “I’m not suggesting let’s open on 52 Sundays. I’m saying, put it in the hands of the retailers, because they know their business best.”

For Merz, the question is economic. Consumer confidence has been in the doldrums since the post-Covid cost of living crisis. Apart from an increase in May, retail sales have been sluggish for more than a year.

But even if reforming Sunday trading is a panacea, he can only take things so far. Much of the decision-making power on this issue rests with state governments – where many politicians are set on the status quo.

Markus Sӧder, the Bavarian premier, who hails from Merz’s political party, was implacable last week. “Nothing will change regarding Sunday rest in my state,” he declared.

Uprooting such an entrenched tradition looks like a tall order. As one expat Reddit user put it: “What was, must be what continued. In Germany, the only thing less flexible than their mindset is the bread.”

data.title: Mango heir’s therapist said: Confront your father on cliff
data.description:
Jonathan Andic, the son of Isak Andic, the billionaire Mango founder, was told by a therapist to confront his father on a cliff edge days before he plunged off a mountain.

In a case that has gripped Spain, Isak Andic, 71, fell 300ft to his death while walking with his 45-year-old son on Montserrat mountain near Barcelona in December 2024.

Jonathan was arrested in May on suspicion of murder. He denies wrongdoing and maintains that his father stumbled while taking a picture of the view.

Police claim there had been tensions between father and son over the running of fashion brand Mango, and a dispute over inheritance plans. They say that the father’s phone was found in his pocket after the fall.

Now, a new witness has told police that a therapist told Jonathan to confront his father on a cliff edge.

The witness, described by the newspaper El Periódico as a middle-aged British female resident in Barcelona, said she had been asked by the Andic family’s therapist to seek out an “extreme route” for the two men to walk together.

The idea allegedly proposed by Maria Julia Lüderwaldt, an alternative therapist who had been coaching Jonathan and Isak, was that they should confront their problems on a precipice.

The British woman told police that Lüderwaldt had instructed Jonathan to place himself and his father in an “extreme situation”, describing an exercise in which he should stand “at the edge of a cliff”, according to La Vanguardia newspaper.

The witness, who works as a translator and did occasional work for Ms Lüderwaldt, said that she had chosen the route in the Montserrat Natural Park, and accompanied Jonathan on walks in the area a few days before his father was found dead at the bottom of a cliff.

Prosecutors have not accused Ms Lüderwaldt of any offence or placed her under investigation, and there is no suggestion that the alleged therapeutic exercise was intended to cause physical harm.

Vehicle tracking determined suspect’s movements

The Mossos d’Esquadra Catalan police force had previously used vehicle tracking to determine that the suspect had visited the same trail on three previous occasions, on Dec 7, 8, and 10.

In her detention order, Judge Raquel Nieto Galván indicated that these dates showed a premeditated plan for what she and prosecutor Teresa Yoldi considered to be a suspected homicide.

The translator reportedly backed up her testimony with messages she had shared with the therapist, and said she had decided to come forward as she was literally “losing sleep” over holding back evidence in a case that has attracted a huge amount of publicity.

In the detention order, Judge Nieto Galván wrote that the main cause for that poor relationship was Jonathan’s “obsession with money”.

Isak’s fortune in 2024 was valued at €4.5bn (£3.9bn).

The money, comprising his 95 per cent stake in Mango plus around £1bn in property assets, was divided equally between his three children, Jonathan, Judith and Sarah, in a settlement in June.

Jonathan continues to protest his innocence and has stood down from his business activities in order to prepare his defence in an eventual trial.

data.title: London Assembly chairman debanked after Ukraine visit
data.description:
A senior Conservative has claimed he was debanked by HSBC-owned First Direct after a visit to Ukraine.

Andrew Boff, the chairman of the London Assembly, said that after he spent £36 on coffees and lunch in Kyiv during a visit in May, he returned to Britain to find that his bank card no longer worked.

He said that on July 8, he received a letter from First Direct informing him that the retail bank would close his accounts in 90 days, on Oct 6.

The letter, seen by The Telegraph, said his bank card had been cancelled and his electronic banking had “ceased”. It read: “We have identified transactions made in a sanctioned country.

“As part of regulations, we must comply with sanction laws and manage any risks eﬀectively. Following a recent review, we determined that the management of these risks is outside of our acceptable limits.

“We’re giving you 90 days written notice that we’ll end your contracts with us. We’ll close the accounts detailed
above, along with any related services, on Oct 6 2026, unless you arrange to close them sooner.”

Ukraine is not considered a high-risk country for money laundering purposes according to the Financial Action Task Force, a G7-founded body which sets international anti-money-laundering standards.

Unlike in other countries, there is no legal right to a bank account in the UK, and banks can choose to close accounts for commercial reasons, as well as if there is suspicious or sanctioned activity.

Mr Boff said: “It is imperative that we have a robust sanctions programme so as to clamp down on the means that the Russian war machine has to fund its brutal war.

“But a robust sanctions programme is one which can differentiate between buying a coffee in Ukraine and a coffee in Russia.”

He added: “I am disappointed that First Direct has been unable to communicate more comprehensively, and that there has been no way for me to clarify to them what has happened with the hope of regaining access to my savings and direct debits. This is simply unacceptable and I hope that the system can be revised before more Britons are caught this way.”

First Direct was launched as the UK’s first telephone-only bank in 1989, set up as a division of HSBC.

The Telegraph has previously heard from humanitarian workers, small business owners, charities and even churches who have had their accounts closed without explanation.

Debanking hit record levels in 2024-25, according to figures from the Financial Conduct Authority. An estimated 453,230 accounts were shut down, data released to The Telegraph under Freedom of Information rules shows.

This was a more than tenfold increase on the 45,091 accounts closed in 2016-17, and an 11pc jump on the 408,000 accounts closed in the 2023-24 tax year.

In all of the cases, lenders cited “financial crime reasons” for the decision to close accounts.

Nigel Farage, who was debanked by private bank Coutts in 2023, said: “Nothing has changed, despite all the promises.”

Last week, Reform accused the National Crime Agency of leaking MPs’ bank details to the media, including transactions which were flagged as suspicious.

Earlier this year, the Government implemented stricter rules for debanking. Victims can now expect 90 days’ notice of an account closure in most cases and better explanations. But banks can still refuse to explain their actions if they believe that doing so would help criminals launder money.

HSBC on behalf of First Direct declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why students aren’t repaying their loans
data.description:
I attended university during the peak of lockdown. I was part of the generation that took out the average maintenance loan of about £7,000 and was told to stay in a single-bed, box-sized room and interact only with a household of 12 people randomly assigned to you.

I couldn’t think of anything worse, so even though I wanted to start my new life, reinvent myself, dye my hair blue, etc. I decided to stay at home.

It was the best decision I made, which I’ve only come to realise now: I saved about seven grand on accommodation fees for the year.

It was the richest I had ever felt. Naturally, I wanted to spend it, but I invested it instead. And, no, I haven’t put the profit into paying off my student loan.

The real issue lies within the mechanism of the entire loan, not just the maintenance allotment. The full student loan is not a standard loan. It acts as a phantom debt that hangs over people, scaring them into a frenzy.

The interest outpaces repayments across the system as a whole. The interest added to student loans last year was more than double the value of everything graduates paid back. It does not touch your credit rating, and it is wiped out entirely 30 years after graduation (40 years for those who started after 2023), no matter how big or, more likely, how small a dent you have made in it.

The Government’s own forecasts suggest that only about a third of my cohort will ever repay the loan in full.

Voluntary overpayment on the loan essentially takes the form of a donation to the Treasury, for a balance that was destined to be written off anyway. It makes more sense to accept it for what it is: the cheapest money you’ll ever be offered, on terms no bank would ever give you. It is a rational choice.

In the 1990s, the first generation of the higher education student funding model was a mortgage-style loan. It performed so badly that the Government sold the final £890m of the book for £160m The equivalent of 18p per £1. Learning almost nothing from this, except that it was possible to just move the losses off the books, we rolled out more loans in a similar fashion. At least in the 1990s the job market was less dire for graduates.

Now, let’s look at Covid. The Bounce Back Loans Scheme pushed £46.5bn out the door with barely a credit check, and the Government’s own repayment data are confession enough. Barely 16 per cent of Bounce Back loans have been repaid in full. A quarter have already been settled by taxpayers under the state guarantee.

Across the Covid loan schemes, the government has paid banks £11.9bn to cover lending losses. Of that, £1.94bn in loans has been flagged as suspected fraud, with £1.56bn of the compensation paid to lenders relating to those cases. This was another government-backed loan scheme that was silently written off, with many business owners viewing it as a grant with extra steps.

You would think that a state which had watched its lending fail twice would change the mechanism the third time. Instead it scaled it up. The student loan book stands at £295bn, is forecast to reach £500bn by the late 2040s and to pass £1tn in cash terms by the 2060s. And when ministers finally did “reform” the system in 2022, look at what they corrected: not the mechanism, but the Treasury’s exposure to it.

The repayment period was extended to 40 years and the income threshold for repayments was reduced. The latest Budget quietly froze that threshold for three years – meaning more borrowers are likely to begin repaying sooner. Fiscal drag is doing the work that the chancellor felt too politically divisive to announce.

Every correction has made the product behave less like a loan and more like a tax, while keeping the label that spares the Government accountability.

And, to what end? Going back to the maintenance loan, despite its name, it fails to provide adequate funding. It now covers barely half of what students need to live on; even those with the maximum support have to rely on the bank of mum and dad or work more than 20 hours a week at a minimum-wage job to reach a basic standard of living.

So students, take out a loan too small to live on, but too big to ever pay off after added interest. To be collected by a mechanism designed not to collect.

data.title: Leon boss attacks ‘unfair’ tax cut for pubs
data.description:
The boss of Leon has accused Andy Burnham of creating a divide within hospitality by cutting business rates for pubs while leaving restaurants and hotels to face soaring tax bills.

John Vincent, who co-founded the company in 2004, said he could not understand why restaurants had been excluded from the Prime Minister’s flagship support package, claiming Labour was treating businesses with identical customers and costs differently.

He told The Telegraph: “We are very confused as to why restaurants are not covered. They are suffering massively, just like pubs. We all compete in the same market, and we have the same cost structures.

“When you have the same customers and the same costs, it is the classic definition of being in the same business, so this creates a false and unfair distinction. We can only hope Andy Burnham has a plan and that there’s more to come.”

Mr Vincent spoke out after the Prime Minister announced a 20pc cut in business rates for pubs, clubs and live music venues from April next year, a move expected to cost around £100m annually.

‘Not a huge amount of money’

Tom Kerridge, who is spearheading calls for a VAT reduction for hospitality venues, welcomed the announcement but added that “it’s not a huge amount of money for each venue”.

The chef and pub owner said: “The average bill is cut by about £1,000, which isn’t hugely significant in the grand scheme of things.”

He added that neighbourhood restaurants also deserved recognition as community hubs: “The biggest thing that will make a big difference to every single space is that reduction in VAT.”

Allen Simpson, the chief executive of UKHospitality, described the announcement as “a welcome first step” but said restaurants were struggling just as much as pubs, while hotels faced average business rates increases of 110pc.

He said: “Today’s announcement will provide welcome support for pubs, clubs and live music venues, but they only account for around a fifth of hospitality jobs. The rest of the sector now needs to see the same ambition.”

Jon Hendry Pickup, the chief executive of Butlin’s, said: “We’re one of the largest employers in the communities this Government wants to support, investing in UK tourism and creating jobs in coastal economies.

“Businesses and resort operators making that contribution shouldn’t be overlooked simply because of their size.”

Stuart Adam, a senior economist at the Institute for Fiscal Studies, criticised the “complex, ever-changing mish-mash of policies” across the business rates system.

He said: “Business rates have gone from being one of the most stable and simple taxes in the system to one of the least. We seem to be moving ever closer to a system in which the Government gives every business a score out of ten each year according to how much it likes the business that year, and sets its tax rate that year accordingly.”

Tax experts said the pubs package was more generous than the headline figure suggested because it built on support already announced by Rachel Reeves earlier this year.

Alex Probyn, of global tax firm Ryan, said the 20pc figure combined with earlier relief amounted to “a significant package” for eligible pubs.

Mr Burnham’s intervention has triggered calls for broader business rates support, well beyond hospitality. The boss of Heathrow, which pays the highest business rates bill in Britain, said he would “love” Mr Burnham to be as sympathetic towards travel as he had been to pubs.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, said its rates bill had jumped by £50m a year under Ms Reeves.

He said: “If they do the same for airports that they’ve done for pubs, it would be really nice. People think travel is important, as important maybe as going to the pub. We don’t want to make it more expensive for them to travel than it already is.

“Taxation has a huge impact on our business and is one of the biggest factors in making our aviation industry less competitive compared to everywhere else in Europe. I don’t think we should add to that. We should try, if we can, to make it more competitive.”

data.title: Burnham’s Energy Secretary demanded Britain hit full net zero within 10 years
data.description:
Andy Burnham’s new Energy Secretary called for Britain to hit net zero within 10 years rather than the current target of reaching net zero by 2050.

Writing in an essay in the Foreign Affairs magazine in 2019, Miatta Fahnbulleh said the UK should try to hit net zero by the mid-2030s at the latest and called for a “global Green New Deal”.

She wrote: “The goal should be net zero carbon emissions within 10 to 15 years, which will require governments to make significant investments in green infrastructure.”

To pay for it, Ms Fahnbulleh has called for wealth taxes and higher borrowing.

Britain is trying to decarbonise its power system by 2030 and achieve net zero across the economy by 2050, meaning no net greenhouse gas emissions.

Achieving this target is already hugely ambitious as it will require the replacement of millions of gas boilers, the shift away from petrol and diesel cars to electric vehicles and the reengineering of large swathes of industry.

In the same 2019 essay, Ms Fahnbulleh called for “quotas for the use of fossil fuel energy” to help tackle climate change. She has also advocated an even tougher windfall tax on oil and gas company profits.

Mr Burnham has signalled that he will look more favourably on fossil fuel exploration in the North Sea, prompting optimism in the oil and gas industry.

However, Ms Fahnbulleh’s past comments suggest she may not soften Labour’s position significantly after replacing Ed Miliband as energy secretary. Opponents argue that the Government is already going too fast on net zero, driving up energy prices and crippling industry in the process.

Before entering Parliament in 2024, Ms Fahnbulleh headed the New Economics Foundation, a Left-leaning think tank, which under her leadership advocated a tax on frequent flyers and lower interest rates for green energy investments.

After her election as MP for Peckham, Ms Fahnbulleh told the 2024 Labour Party conference that energy was too expensive because of “our over-reliance on international fossil fuel markets”.

She has also been a keen supporter of the windfall tax, which imposes a total tax take of 78pc on any profits made from producing fossil fuels in UK waters.

In 2022, when the Conservatives first imposed the Labour-inspired levy, Ms Fahnbulleh called for them to go further and strip tax breaks given to oil and gas companies which support investment.

The new Energy Secretary has also backed the idea of charging higher prices to households that use more energy, a policy that would penalise those with large families and bigger homes.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Miatta Fahnbulleh, remarkably, seems to have drunk even more of the green lobby Kool-Aid than Ed Miliband.

“The first thing she should do is get out of her ivory tower and on to a plane to speak to the workers in Aberdeen who are losing their livelihoods thanks to her policies.”

Richard Tice, Reform UK’s spokesman for energy, said: “The new Energy Secretary is another eco zealot who will be as bad as the old. Obsessed by net zero, clueless about real economics, our energy costs will rise and the grid will become less stable. The nation will pay a heavy price for this mistake.”

Ms Fahnbulleh served as a junior minister in the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero between June 2024 and July 2025.

In that role, she led the Warm Homes Plan – a £15bn scheme to install insulation, heat pumps and solar panels in the homes of low-income families to boost the energy efficiency of the properties.

A government spokesman said: “Net zero is the economic opportunity of the 21st century. We’ve secured £100bn in private investment into clean energy this parliament. As the Energy Secretary said, her focus is bringing bills down for good.”

data.title: Mike Ashley raises stake in Hugo Boss after £2.3bn takeover bid
data.description:
Mike Ashley’s Frasers has upped its stake in Hugo Boss after the retail billionaire tabled a £2.3bn takeover bid for the German fashion house.

Frasers confirmed on Tuesday that it had bought a further 3.7pc of the shares in Hugo Boss, taking its stake above 30pc, the legal threshold for launching a formal takeover offer under German stock market rules.

The FTSE 100 retailer announced an offer last month to acquire the remaining share capital of the German luxury label as it indicated it would continue to build its holding.

The board of Hugo Boss rejected Mr Ashley’s initial approach at €38 (£32) per share earlier this month, insisting the bid was “inadequate” and undervalued the brand.

In a market announcement on Tuesday morning, Frasers said it was “pleased to confirm that it has exceeded the mandatory bid threshold of 30pc as provided for in the German Takeover Code”.

“The offer of €38 per Hugo Boss share remains open for shareholders to accept.”

Frasers has previously said its bid was final. It is the lowest offer Frasers was able to make under Frankfurt’s market rules. Hugo Boss shares have traded between €37.90 and €38 in recent weeks, following Frasers’ approach.

Shareholders in Hugo Boss, which is promoted by Sir David Beckham and Naomi Campbell, have until July 27 to accept the proposed deal. The German brand’s stock has halved over the past three years.

Stephan Sturm, Hugo Boss’s chairman, described the bid earlier this month as “financially inadequate and [it] fails to appropriately reflect Hugo Boss’s value and future potential”.

The increase in its stake represents the latest piece of empire-building for Mr Ashley’s Frasers, the group that also controls Sports Direct and Jack Wills.

Mr Ashley controls 74pc of Frasers, formerly known as Sports Direct before a takeover of House of Fraser that saved the department store from collapse.

The billionaire has spent recent years building up a portfolio of fashion, sportswear and luxury brands.

Frasers has become one of the most active buyers of struggling retailers, acquiring House of Fraser in 2018 after the department store chain collapsed into administration, before adding brands including Missguided and I Saw It First.

The group has said it wanted to create a “house of brands” spanning different parts of the consumer market, from its core Sports Direct business through to higher-end fashion labels.

Mr Ashley has also built stakes in a number of retailers and consumer businesses, often positioning Frasers as a potential buyer or strategic partner as companies face pressure from changing consumer habits and rising costs.

data.title: Net zero fuel rules must be relaxed, says easyJet boss
data.description:
The boss of easyJet has called on Andy Burnham to relax rules around net zero fuel for airlines.

Kenton Jarvis, easyJet’s chief executive, said mandates for airlines to use sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) were too stretching and would have to be pushed back.

There is a particular concern around supplies of eSAF, a synthetic fuel that the Government says airlines must begin using alongside regular SAF derived from waste cooking oil from 2028.

Mr Jarvis said: “We do support measures to decarbonise but in the real world, if eSAF isn’t available, it’s pretty pointless having a mandate to buy something that doesn’t exist.

“It’s available in laboratories and beakers, which won’t fly planes. We’re going to have to push the mandate back.”

Just one refinery out of 600 worldwide currently produces the substance.

The International Air Transport Association estimates UK airlines will face a bill of more than £400m if they can locate sufficient eSAF or the same in fines if they cannot.

The plea for a relaxation comes after Miatta Fahnbulleh replaced Ed Miliband as Energy Secretary. However, there are signs she will take a similar hardline stance to climate policy as her predecessor.

Ms Fahnbulleh called for taxes on regular travellers while chief of the New Economics Foundation between 2017 and 2023. During her time there, the think tank proposed adding a levy to tickets that would increase for every subsequent flight taken within a year.

Mr Jarvis would not comment on that specific idea but said: “When you look at surveys, the number one in discretionary spend tends to be travel to go and explore new cities or to go and enjoy some time with your family.”

He added: “What I would say to the Government is that aviation is an important economic enabler. It enables growth and the government is looking for growth.”

The comments came as easyJet’s soaring jet fuel bill cut its profits by more than 70pc after the Iran war caused oil prices to spike.

The UK’s biggest low-cost airline said its pre-tax profits fell to £85m in the three months to the end of June, down from £286m a year earlier.

The decline was driven by a £105m increase in jet fuel costs and weaker demand from flyers after the outbreak of the conflict in February.

The airline said a slowdown in bookings from passengers nervous about potential fuel shortages outweighed a recovery in last-minute bookings later in the quarter.

However, revenues still rose 2pc to £3bn, while its package holiday business delivered profits of £84m, broadly unchanged from a year earlier, with customer numbers up 8pc.

Shares climbed around 1pc on Thursday. They dropped 12pc on Wednesday as the Middle East conflict pushed oil prices above $96 per barrel.

The update comes just more than a week after easyJet agreed to a £5.7bn takeover by private equity giant Apollo, which trumped a rival approach from US private credit firm Castlelake.

Apollo has offered 715p a share, an 81pc premium to the company’s share price before takeover interest emerged. Apollo has until Aug 7 to make a firm offer under UK takeover rules.

The takeover battle followed a sharp sell-off in easyJet’s shares earlier this year as Donald Trump’s strikes on Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz drove up fuel costs across the aviation industry.

Mr Jarvis said: “We have continued to manage the impact of the Middle East conflict and its effect on fuel prices and booking trends during the quarter.

“As consumer confidence increases, we are seeing the load factor gap close for peak summer as customers continue to prioritise travel.”

Passenger numbers were broadly unchanged at 25.8 million in the three months to the end of June, although the proportion of seats filled fell from 90.2pc to 88.9pc.

EasyJet said it had sold 68pc of seats for the summer period, with ticket prices broadly flat compared with last year. It said booking trends had improved since May as more customers made late reservations.

data.title: Ageing nuclear plants given life extension to keep Britain’s lights on
data.description:
Two ageing nuclear power stations will stay operational until 2030 to help keep Britain’s lights on.

EDF said on Wednesday it had decided to extend the life of the Hartlepool and Heysham 1 plants for an extra two years after safety inspections of their cores.

The sites have a combined capacity of about 2.3 gigawatts, enough to power around four million homes.

Hartlepool and Heysham 1 are two of four advanced gas-cooled reactors (AGRs) that have been generating since the 1980s.

The others, Heysham 2 and Torness, also had their lifespans extended until March 2030 last year.

EDF said those dates remain “under review” and will be subject to further safety extensions, leaving open the possibility that the plants could run for even longer.

John Munro, the head of nuclear operations at EDF, said: “The AGR stations have been the backbone of secure, zero carbon generation in the UK for five decades.

“The nuclear fleet also has a key role in maintaining grid stability, which is so important as the overall system becomes led by renewables.

“Today’s decision retains stable baseload, domestic generation, reducing reliance on imports during tighter periods.”

Ministers have said “baseload” power from nuclear plants will play an important stabilising role as the grid switches to more variable sources of green energy such as solar and wind.

Risk of a ‘nuclear gap’

The extension was also welcomed by British Gas owner Centrica, which owns a 20pc stake in the AGR fleet.

It means Britain’s total nuclear capacity is on course to stay at around 6.1 gigawatts until at least the start of the next decade.

However, the country still potentially faces a “nuclear gap” from 2030 if the AGRs come offline.

That would leave Sizewell B, in Suffolk, as the sole remaining plant, before Hinkley Point C in Somerset and Sizewell C come online later in the 2030s.

EDF said that each year a nuclear plant is kept open, national demand for expensive liquefied natural gas cargoes is reduced by about 2.5 billion cubic metres – equivalent to some 20pc of annual imports.

Mr Munro added: “When nuclear goes offline it is often replaced with gas generation.

“If we are serious about reducing the UK’s reliance on gas and retaining system stability, it makes sense to keep these stations online to support energy security and our carbon targets.”

EDF also recently agreed a lifetime extension for Sizewell B with the Office for Nuclear Regulation that will see the plant, the last one commissioned in Britain in 1995, run until 2055.

Seven AGR power plants were in operation originally when EDF bought them in 2009. It has already shut down Hunterston B, Hinkley Point B and Dungeness B.

How long the remaining four will continue depends on the condition of their graphite reactor cores, EDF has previously said.

Hairline cracks can occur in the graphite over time, potentially impeding control rods which must be dropped to lower output – or shut the reactor down in emergencies.

To continue operating, EDF must prove to regulators that this will not happen and that it could still shut down the reactors during a “one in 10,000 years” earthquake, much larger than any the UK has ever experienced.

data.title: Eurostar locked in passenger row with St Pancras
data.description:
Eurostar is locked in a row with London St Pancras International over fears that surging passenger numbers will push the station to breaking point.

Gwendoline Cazenave, Eurostar’s chief executive, said she was at loggerheads with station bosses over how best to cope with a significant increase in trains using the Channel tunnel over the next few years.

Unless more space is freed up for millions more passengers a year, the influx will lead to a meltdown at St Pancras, where people already have to queue to complete EU border formalities, she told The Telegraph.

St Pancras is the UK terminus for Eurostar, carrying passengers to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam.

The Grade I-listed station is set for significant growth with the rollout of double-decker trains, known as Eurostar Celestia and new operators such as Virgin bringing millions more passengers over the next decade.

However, London St Pancras Highspeed, which runs the station, is declining to fund upgrades proposed by Eurostar and put forward its own plans instead, which it says will slash waiting times.

Ms Cazenave said both sides were locked in disagreement.

“The big question we have with our St Pancras Highspeed friends is about our plans for a bigger footprint in the station,” she said.

“The major challenge for a cross-border train is the border itself and the customer experience of the border, and we need space for this. People who have travelled with us will know that in such a small space, you exit the UK, then you enter Schengen.

“This is about the bigger view for British customers and British growth. We need a longer-term vision beyond 2040 and the end of the concession of St Pancras Highspeed,” she added.

Since a major refurbishment two decades ago, London St Pancras – which was saved from demolition in the 1960s by John Betjeman, the poet and long-time Telegraph contributor – has become one of Britain’s most well-known stations.

However, travellers to and from the Continent face poorly laid-out departure and arrival halls.

Eurostar passengers arriving from the EU must exit the station by descending via escalators to the floor below, which also houses departing passengers.

Having both sets of passengers on the lower level means space is cramped, with travellers often forced to stand in Eurostar’s departure lounge while they wait for their trains.

Under Eurostar’s plan, people arriving at St Pancras from the Continent would exit through the front of the station instead of going to the lower level.

The change would vastly expand the space available on the lower floor for British and French border control facilities and travellers waiting to board their train, Ms Cazenave said.

“We are working on additional space, but don’t exactly have the same views. We want to use the upstairs area, where there’s a big space, a huge area of square metres, for arrivals,” she said.

“The first time I ever went to St Pancras, I said there’s no space problem here. You would come straight out onto the road of St Pancras and not need to go down into the station. Then you can use the whole place downstairs for borders.

Instead, London St Pancras Highspeed has proposed a series of incremental improvements that it says would increase passengers going through security from 2,500 an hour to 5,000 without the need for disruptive infrastructure work.

The £100m plan would include turning queues sideways and extending them into the arrivals area, doubling the space for security checks so that passengers can board in 30 minutes. The Eurostar proposal would add a further £50m to the bill.

Ms Cazenave warned that the station’s stance will lead to chaotic scenes once the double-deckers – carrying more than 1,000 passengers – enter service in 2031. By this time, Sir Richard Branson’s Virgin and Italy’s Trenitalia may be operating a dozen trains apiece.

Ms Cazenave said the risk of a logjam could also frustrate Eurostar’s plans to launch services to Frankfurt and Geneva and that the row was doubly frustrating because Eurostar was spending £1.7bn on the new trains.

She said: “We keep saying that it’s a golden era for international railways and the fact that there are new operators coming means that we’re right.

“We have put money on the table for new train sets because we believe in the market and that it’s going to grow.

“We are planning to go to Frankfurt and Geneva and we are working to answer the regulator’s expectations about sharing our depot with Virgin. But we need a project and a financing framework beyond 2040.”

London St Pancras Highspeed said it had been in discussions with Eurostar, the border authorities, the Department for Transport and Historic England for the past two years about plans to double passenger capacity at St Pancras, and would commence the detailed design phase in the autumn.

“Demand for more sustainable international travel is increasing and we remain focused on getting the station ready to meet it,” a London St Pancras Highspeed spokesman said.

Ms Cazenave, who will have been chief executive for four years in September, said she was also hopeful that Andy Burnham, the presumptive prime minister, would try to tap into the potential of the Channel tunnel.

“We need to think big,” she said. “I’m not talking about money. I’m talking about rules, planning, space, business models, joining issues up rather than looking at them in isolation.

“There are several key topics on which we need a policy framework. Those are depot capacity, station incapacity, border fluidity and ways of working, the tax regime, regulation.

“All of these need a political vision which is consistent and connected to push this growth in sustainable travel. That is what I am expecting from them.”

Given sufficient backing, Eurostar has the potential to make deep inroads into travel markets dominated by airlines rather than rail journeys of up to six hours, Ms Cazenave said, citing the success of the London-Amsterdam route.

Services which launched in 2018 were transformed last year following a major investment in new border facilities and lounges at Amsterdam’s Centraal station.

Since then, Eurostar has expanded its timetable to five trains a day, allowing it to double passenger numbers and capture more than 35pc of the overall London-Amsterdam travel market.

This makes it the biggest player before airlines, including KLM, the Dutch flag carrier.

“Amsterdam has been an amazing success,” she said. “People want to travel comfortably, city centre to city centre, and sustainably. And we can see that time on the train rather than in the airport is really useful for getting work done.”

She said that, thanks to the location of French officials at St Pancras, Eurostar had not suffered the same disruption as airports from the introduction of the new EU entry and exit scheme, which requires the collection of fingerprints and facial scans from non-EU citizens.

“We are very close to the border police and when they see that it’s a peak and it’s going to be complicated, they are flexible,” she said.

On other routes, including London to Paris and Brussels, Eurostar has an 85pc market share, having won over the vast majority of passengers who are not taking onward flights.

Cazenave said she was not frightened by the prospect of taking on Virgin, which plans to launch Channel tunnel services in 2030, ending what by then will have been a 36-year Eurostar monopoly.

“When you’re a CEO that is not possible,” she said, adding that Eurostar’s ability to continue to offer “an understated, elegant experience” will also depend on a satisfactory outcome to the wrangling over St Pancras.

“This is really what we are working on,” she said. “What we want to do with Eurostar Celestia is to keep this seamless, smooth and caring experience.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Asda’s stores are a disaster. What made it think it could build homes?
data.description:
The fridge is empty, the front door is hanging off and there’s an “out of order” sign outside the toilet.

Welcome to “Asda Homes”, the housing development that nobody wanted but is going ahead anyway, proof – if any were needed – that those running Britain’s third-largest supermarket are scraping the very bottom of the biscuit barrel for ideas on how to stop the great Asda slow-motion car crash.

In what sounds like one of the most depressing business concepts ever to make it out of a boardroom, Asda executives are forging ahead with plans to turn 10 acres of prime London land into flats. More than 1,500 to be precise, across a series of tower blocks stretching 35 storeys high.

The site, in Ealing, south-west London, will come with a new 140,000 sq ft Asda superstore where lucky residents can buy their beans – providing there are any on the shelves of course – and a “community hub” – whatever that means.

If Asda’s shares were publicly traded, this plan would be the mother of all sell signals.

After five ruinous years of private equity ownership, the chain is in desperate need of reinvigoration centred on the absolute basics of grocery retail, not some half-baked foray into house-building, a sector that it knows nothing whatsoever about.

It’s not just that the Ealing concept sounds decidedly substandard. It also risks being a waste of time and energy, not to mention a distraction at a time when management should be laser-focused on turning around a core grocery business that has been going backwards at a startling rate.

There’s already enough to sort out on the shop floor. Allan Leighton, the executive chairman, himself has been up front about that.

He said in March that the supermarket giant still has “plenty to do” in its turnaround after posting another slump in sales and earnings.

Leighton deserves credit for not shying away from the company’s countless challenges, but his assessment risks being the understatement of the century.

Asda revealed it crashed to a near £1bn annual loss last month, a low that was largely pinned on the astonishing bill for one of the most catastrophic IT overhauls ever seen in corporate Britain.

The tech issues have at times contributed to the stocking problems it has suffered.

But it’s not the main reason why the chain has so spectacularly surrendered swathes of market share to rivals. That’s down to old-fashioned shopkeeping – or rather a lack of it.

Having knocked Morrisons out of fourth place in 2022, Aldi is expected to take third spot from Asda in the coming years. It lags behind in key categories such as alcohol, pet food and toiletries, but is already ahead in food and drink. Lidl is rapidly catching up too.

Before it was purchased by TDR Capital and the Issa brothers in a junk bond-financed £6.8bn deal in 2020, Asda held 14.8pc of the grocery market.

Today it is 11.5pc, the lowest it has been since that auspicious occasion, which left a company employing 146,000 people at the time, saddled with nearly £4bn of expensive debt.

TDR’s buyout has proven to be both a massive impediment to the sort of investment blitz that Asda is crying out for to see off the competition, and a major distraction from the day-to-day business of selling potatoes and loo roll.

The poor souls in Asda’s press department who are charged with somehow putting a positive spin on all this are fond of pointing out how the supermarket is sitting on a large cash pile – £1.3bn at the last count – as if that’s proof that it can cope with its spiralling borrowing costs.

But all it does is shine an unflattering light on how much cash the company would have if it wasn’t for all the money that was going out of the door to service its mammoth debt pile.

The Telegraph recently totted up the numbers, and Asda has spent a truly eye-popping £2.2bn servicing the loans taken out to finance TDR’s takeover – money that could otherwise be spent sprucing up its stores and fighting a price war.

Asda’s hefty borrowings are a permanent albatross around its neck, and they’re getting more expensive every year. Management is also proud of a refinancing that it pulled off in 2024, which pushed out any major debt repayments by several years.

Yet, what they are less forthcoming about is the heavy price it paid in the form of much higher interest payments. The cost of Asda’s debt burden surged by a fifth last year to a staggering £730m, up from £611m the previous year.

A lighter debt load would not only mean more to invest in some seriously shabby stores but also to fight a price war that Leighton ignited. Many in the industry think he will struggle to win against competitors that don’t have the same balance-sheet constraints.

It may explain why Asda’s market share has continued to go backwards since he arrived, but also why it has been forced into a heavy cost-cutting drive that has weighed heavily on the supermarket’s battle-weary workforce.

Even without Asda’s myriad challenges, an attempted move into housing would be extremely difficult to pull off. Surely the chain’s management is aware of that?

You would think the recent experience of John Lewis would be enough to convince Leighton and his team to pull the plug.

The mutual’s big housebuilding dream was dead after just a few years, crushed by a lethal combination of planning red tape and spiralling costs. Why would it be any different for Asda?

At least it has convinced housing developer Barratt to shoulder the upfront costs.

Yet more than two years after the Ealing scheme was first conceived, it still hasn’t received planning consent. Meanwhile, there is opposition from the NHS and Historic England has raised concerns about the plans. 

It sounds like a headache Asda could well do without.

The retail industry is again beginning to speculate about the prospect of a mega merger between Asda and Morrisons, which is also languishing under the heavy burden of private-equity ownership.

Yet, that has all the feel of two drunks propping each other up at the bar, and there is no guarantee regulators would give it their blessing.

Is Asda’s unlikely housebuilding pivot an admission from executives that they’ve run out of ideas, and road?
